abstract: Embodied long-horizon manipulation requires robotic systems to process multimodal inputs-such as vision and natural language-and translate them into executable actions. However, existing learning-based approaches often depend on large, task-specific datasets and struggle to generalize to unseen scenarios. Recent methods have explored using large language models (LLMs) as high-level planners that decompose tasks into subtasks using natural language and guide pretrained low-level controllers. Yet, these approaches assume perfect execution from low-level policies, which is unrealistic in real-world environments with noise or suboptimal behaviors. To overcome this, we fully discard the pretrained low-level policy and instead use the LLM to directly generate executable code plans within a closed-loop framework. Our planner employs chain-of-thought (CoT)-guided few-shot learning with incrementally structured examples to produce robust and generalizable task plans. Complementing this, a reporter evaluates outcomes using RGB-D and delivers structured feedback, enabling recovery from misalignment and replanning under partial observability. This design eliminates per-step inference, reduces computational overhead, and limits error accumulation that was observed in previous methods. Our framework achieves state-of-the-art performance on 30+ diverse seen and unseen long-horizon tasks across LoHoRavens, CALVIN, Franka Kitchen, and cluttered real-world settings.

keyInsights: Direct LLM-based code generation, coupled with a robust closed-loop feedback mechanism, can effectively replace data-intensive pretrained low-level policies for long-horizon robotic manipulation, achieving high performance and data-agnosticism.

Structured, incrementally difficult few-shot examples augmented with Chain-of-Thought reasoning significantly enhance an LLM's ability to generalize to novel and complex tasks by guiding its logical understanding.

VLM-based structured contextual feedback is critical for real-world reliability, enabling the robotic system to detect errors, adapt to partial observability (e.g., occluded objects), and iteratively replan for successful task completion.
originalProblem: Traditional Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models require massive, costly datasets for pretraining and fine-tuning, limiting their scalability and generalization to novel robotic tasks.

Hierarchical frameworks that use LLMs for high-level planning and pre-trained low-level policies often struggle with real-world noise and perturbations due to the 'perfect execution' assumption of these policies, leading to cumulative errors.

Existing LLM-based code generation methods for robotics typically operate in an open-loop manner, lacking robust feedback for error recovery, and rely on randomly selected examples for few-shot learning, which is ineffective for complex generalization.
results: DAHLIA achieved state-of-the-art success rates on the LoHoRavens benchmark, including 100% on five original tasks and consistently highest performance on 10 newly generated, unseen tasks, with closed-loop feedback improving performance by up to 24% over its planner-only variant.

The framework demonstrated strong scalability and generalization across different robot embodiments (UR5e and Franka arm) and task setups (CALVIN and Franka Kitchen benchmarks), achieving 100% success in several tasks and up to 8% improvements in precise dexterous manipulation.

In real-world demonstrations, DAHLIA successfully completed four challenging tasks, including handling an initially occluded object by detecting its exposure via the VLM reporter and initiating successful re-planning, showcasing robust error recovery and adaptability to dynamic environments.
solution: DAHLIA introduces a dual-tunnel closed-loop framework where a 'Planner' (LLM) generates executable Python code directly from human instructions, while a 'Reporter' (VLM) provides structured contextual feedback after each execution loop.

It implements Chain-of-Thought (CoT)-guided few-shot adaptation using an incrementally structured curriculum of examples, teaching the LLM robust reasoning patterns for better generalization to unseen tasks.

The framework leverages temporal abstraction by executing entire sequences of generated motion primitives within a loop before seeking feedback, thereby reducing per-step inference overhead and limiting error accumulation.
paper_summary.summary: The DAHLIA framework enables robots to perform complex, multi-step manipulation tasks by generating executable code directly from natural language instructions. It employs a closed-loop dual-tunnel system with incremental few-shot adaptation, achieving high success rates on various benchmarks and demonstrating robust error recovery in real-world scenarios without relying on extensive pre-trained policies.

title: Embodied Long Horizon Manipulation with Closed-loop Code Generation and Incremental Few-shot Adaptation

abstract: World models paired with model predictive control (MPC) can be trained offline on large-scale datasets of expert trajectories and enable generalization to a wide range of planning tasks at inference time. Compared to traditional MPC procedures, which rely on slow search algorithms or on iteratively solving optimization problems exactly, gradient-based planning offers a computationally efficient alternative. However, the performance of gradient-based planning has thus far lagged behind that of other approaches. In this paper, we propose improved methods for training world models that enable efficient gradient-based planning. We begin with the observation that although a world model is trained on a next-state prediction objective, it is used at test-time to instead estimate a sequence of actions. The goal of our work is to close this train-test gap. To that end, we propose train-time data synthesis techniques that enable significantly improved gradient-based planning with existing world models. At test time, our approach outperforms or matches the classical gradient-free cross-entropy method (CEM) across a variety of object manipulation and navigation tasks in 10% of the time budget.

organization_info.name: The University of Texas at Austin

keyInsights: Actively addressing the train-test gap through either online data expansion or adversarial robustness training is critical for unlocking the full potential of gradient-based planning.
Adversarial training can effectively smooth the action-level optimization landscape, making gradient-based methods more robust and efficient for continuous control tasks.

Combining powerful self-supervised visual encoders with specialized finetuning of latent dynamics models provides a robust framework for high-performance and computationally efficient robotic planning.
originalProblem: World models suffer from a "train-test gap" where they encounter out-of-distribution latent states during gradient-based planning, leading to compounding prediction errors.
The planning objective, derived from learned world models, often presents a complex, non-smooth loss landscape that hinders the effectiveness of gradient-based optimization.
Gradient-based planning has historically underperformed search-based methods empirically, despite its potential for computational efficiency in model-based control.
results: OWM and AWM substantially improved gradient-based planning success rates by up to +30% in open-loop settings, with AWM+Adam+MPC matching or exceeding the performance of search-based CEM.
The enhanced GBP performance was achieved with an order of magnitude (10x) reduction in computation time compared to the Cross-Entropy Method (CEM).

The finetuning methods effectively closed the train-test gap, reducing the world model error on planning trajectories and demonstrably smoothing the planning loss landscape for more stable optimization.
solution: **Online World Modeling (OWM)** iteratively expands the world model's training data by executing GBP-generated action sequences in the true environment simulator and adding the resulting "corrected" ground-truth trajectories.
**Adversarial World Modeling (AWM)** finetunes the world model by training it on adversarially perturbed latent states and actions, improving robustness and smoothing the planning loss landscape.
The approach leverages a fixed, pretrained DINOv2 self-supervised visual encoder to embed observations into a latent space, with a Vision Transformer serving as the learnable latent world model.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Columbia University and NYU introduced Online World Modeling (OWM) and Adversarial World Modeling (AWM) to mitigate the train-test gap in world models for gradient-based planning (GBP). These methods enabled GBP to achieve performance comparable to or better than search-based planning algorithms like CEM, while simultaneously reducing computation time by an order of magnitude across various robotic tasks.

title: Closing the Train-Test Gap in World Models for Gradient-Based Planning

abstract: A long-standing challenge in AI is to develop agents capable of solving a wide range of physical tasks and generalizing to new, unseen tasks and environments. A popular recent approach involves training a world model from state-action trajectories and subsequently use it with a planning algorithm to solve new tasks. Planning is commonly performed in the input space, but a recent family of methods has introduced planning algorithms that optimize in the learned representation space of the world model, with the promise that abstracting irrelevant details yields more efficient planning. In this work, we characterize models from this family as JEPA-WMs and investigate the technical choices that make algorithms from this class work. We propose a comprehensive study of several key components with the objective of finding the optimal approach within the family. We conducted experiments using both simulated environments and real-world robotic data, and studied how the model architecture, the training objective, and the planning algorithm affect planning success. We combine our findings to propose a model that outperforms two established baselines, DINO-WM and V-JEPA-2-AC, in both navigation and manipulation tasks. Code, data and checkpoints are available at this https URL.

keyInsights: DINO-style visual encoders consistently outperform V-JEPA encoders due to their superior fine-grained object segmentation capabilities, which are essential for precise manipulation and navigation.

While gradient-based planners excel on tasks with smooth cost landscapes, population-based methods like CEM and Nevergrad are more robust for complex, multi-modal cost landscapes encountered in robotics.

The benefits of model scaling (larger encoders and deeper predictors) are primarily observed in complex real-world data, whereas they offer limited or negative gains in simpler simulated environments.
originalProblem: A lack of comprehensive understanding regarding the specific technical choices that drive success in physical planning using Joint-Embedding Predictive World Models (JEPA-WMs).
Prior JEPA-WM implementations often rely on ad-hoc design choices, hindering principled advancement and optimization for robotic tasks.
The challenge of developing AI agents that can efficiently solve a wide range of physical tasks and generalize to unseen environments.
results: The proposed optimal JEPA-WM models consistently outperformed established baselines like DINO-WM and V-JEPA-2-AC across most simulated and real-world environments.
Proprioceptive input consistently improved planning success, and a 2-step rollout loss was generally optimal for simulated tasks, while a 6-step rollout was best for complex real-world dynamics.
Nevergrad, a tuning-free planner, performed comparably to CEM on real-world manipulation data (DROID), offering a practical advantage by requiring less hyperparameter tuning.
solution: Formalize JEPA-WMs as a unified recipe, systematically varying and evaluating key components across model architecture, training objectives, and planning algorithms.
Conduct a component-wise empirical study on diverse simulated (Metaworld, Push-T, Wall, PointMaze, Robocasa) and real-world (DROID) robotic tasks to isolate the impact of each design choice.
Propose optimized JEPA-WM configurations based on the comprehensive analysis, distinguishing between optimal settings for simulated versus complex real-world dynamics.
paper_summary.summary: This research provides a systematic empirical study of Joint-Embedding Predictive World Models (JEPA-WMs), identifying the specific technical choices that contribute to their success in physical planning. It proposes optimized model configurations that consistently achieve higher success rates compared to existing baselines on diverse simulated and real-world robotic manipulation and navigation tasks.

title: What Drives Success in Physical Planning with Joint-Embedding Predictive World Models?

abstract:
Model Predictive Control (MPC) is a widely adopted control paradigm that
leverages predictive models to estimate future system states and optimize
control inputs accordingly. However, while MPC excels in planning and control,
it lacks the capability for environmental perception, leading to failures in
complex and unstructured scenarios. To address this limitation, we introduce
Vision-Language Model Predictive Control (VLMPC), a robotic manipulation
planning framework that integrates the perception power of vision-language
models (VLMs) with MPC. VLMPC utilizes a conditional action sampling module
that takes a goal image or language instruction as input and leverages VLM to
generate candidate action sequences. These candidates are fed into a video
prediction model that simulates future frames based on the actions. In
addition, we propose an enhanced variant, Traj-VLMPC, which replaces video
prediction with motion trajectory generation to reduce computational complexity
while maintaining accuracy. Traj-VLMPC estimates motion dynamics conditioned on
the candidate actions, offering a more efficient alternative for long-horizon
tasks and real-time applications. Both VLMPC and Traj-VLMPC select the optimal
action sequence using a VLM-based hierarchical cost function that captures both
pixel-level and knowledge-level consistency between the current observation and
the task input. We demonstrate that both approaches outperform existing
state-of-the-art methods on public benchmarks and achieve excellent performance
in various real-world robotic manipulation tasks. Code is available at
this https URL

keyInsights: VLMs can effectively guide trajectory optimization by providing spatial understanding and task-relevant knowledge
Trajectory-based approaches are more computationally efficient than frame-by-frame video prediction methods
Dynamic cost function switching based on visual feedback improves task completion success
originalProblem: Traditional Model Predictive Control (MPC) lacks environmental perception capabilities and relies heavily on accurate dynamic models
Existing approaches using language models for robotics focus mainly on high-level task planning rather than trajectory generation and control
results: Achieved 85% success rate on real-world manipulation tasks with novel objects
Demonstrated improved collision avoidance and completion time compared to baseline methods
Enabled long-horizon task execution without requiring pre-defined motion primitives
Showed effective generalization to unseen objects and environmental conditions
solution: Integration of VLMs with MPC to enable perception-aware trajectory planning and control
Development of Traj-VLMPC that uses VLM-driven Gaussian Mixture Models for efficient trajectory sampling and 3D value maps for trajectory assessment
paper_summary.summary: Shandong University and University of Manchester researchers develop a framework combining Vision-Language Models (VLMs) with Model Predictive Control for robotic manipulation, enabling robots to plan and execute complex tasks through trajectory optimization while achieving 85% success rate on real-world manipulation tasks without pre-defined motion primitives.

title: Vision-Language Model Predictive Control for Manipulation Planning and  Trajectory Generation

abstract: Specifying robotic manipulation tasks in a manner that is both expressive and precise remains a central challenge. While visual goals provide a compact and unambiguous task specification, existing goal-conditioned policies often struggle with long-horizon manipulation due to their reliance on single-step action prediction without explicit modeling of task progress. We propose Act2Goal, a general goal-conditioned manipulation policy that integrates a goal-conditioned visual world model with multi-scale temporal control. Given a current observation and a target visual goal, the world model generates a plausible sequence of intermediate visual states that captures long-horizon structure. To translate this visual plan into robust execution, we introduce Multi-Scale Temporal Hashing (MSTH), which decomposes the imagined trajectory into dense proximal frames for fine-grained closed-loop control and sparse distal frames that anchor global task consistency. The policy couples these representations with motor control through end-to-end cross-attention, enabling coherent long-horizon behavior while remaining reactive to local disturbances. Act2Goal achieves strong zero-shot generalization to novel objects, spatial layouts, and environments. We further enable reward-free online adaptation through hindsight goal relabeling with LoRA-based finetuning, allowing rapid autonomous improvement without external supervision. Real-robot experiments demonstrate that Act2Goal improves success rates from 30% to 90% on challenging out-of-distribution tasks within minutes of autonomous interaction, validating that goal-conditioned world models with multi-scale temporal control provide structured guidance necessary for robust long-horizon manipulation. Project page: this https URL

keyInsights: Integrating a goal-conditioned visual world model with multi-scale temporal control enables robust long-horizon manipulation by effectively balancing global task consistency and local reactivity.

Policies trained on large-scale demonstration data, when combined with a structured visual planning mechanism, can achieve strong zero-shot generalization to unseen manipulation tasks and environments.
Reward-free, lightweight online adaptation through HER and LoRA fine-tuning provides a practical path for autonomous skill improvement in real-world scenarios without explicit supervision.
originalProblem: Existing goal-conditioned policies struggle with long-horizon manipulation due to a lack of explicit task progress modeling and understanding of intermediate steps.
Robot policies often exhibit limited generalization to novel objects, environments, or task variations when trained solely on offline demonstration data.
Online adaptation for robotic policies typically requires extensive human supervision, complex reward engineering, or frequent retraining of large models.
results: Act2Goal achieved superior zero-shot generalization, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art baselines in both simulation (e.g., 0.43 vs 0.06 success rate on Robotwin 2.0 Hard mode Pick Bottles) and real-world manipulation tasks.

The online autonomous improvement mechanism led to up to an 8x increase in success rate in some simulated scenarios and boosted real-world out-of-domain task performance (e.g., Plug-In operation from 0.30 to 0.90).

Multi-Scale Temporal Hashing (MSTH) was critical for long-horizon tasks, demonstrating substantially higher success rates (e.g., 0.90-0.95 vs 0.10-0.35) for complex real-world word writing compared to fixed-horizon baselines.
solution: A goal-conditioned visual world model generates multi-scale visual plans, forecasting intermediate states between the current observation and the desired goal.
Multi-Scale Temporal Hashing (MSTH) balances long-horizon consistency with local reactivity by structuring imagined trajectories into dense proximal and sparse distal segments.
A reward-free online autonomous improvement mechanism, utilizing Hindsight Experience Replay and LoRA-based fine-tuning, enables efficient self-supervised adaptation.
paper_summary.summary: Act2Goal proposes a general goal-conditioned robotic policy integrating a vision-based world model for multi-scale planning and a reward-free online adaptation mechanism. The system exhibited strong zero-shot generalization in both simulated and real-world manipulation tasks, with autonomous improvements boosting success rates up to 8x in some simulations and from 0.30 to 0.90 in real-world unseen scenarios.

title: Act2Goal: From World Model To General Goal-conditioned Policy

abstract:
Real-world embodied agents face long-horizon tasks, characterized by
high-level goals demanding multi-step solutions beyond single actions.
Successfully navigating these requires both high-level task planning (i.e.,
decomposing goals into sub-tasks) and low-level motion control (i.e.,
generating precise robot actions). While existing vision language action (VLA)
models and hierarchical architectures offer potential in embodied tasks, the
former often falter in planning, and the latter can suffer from coordination
issues, both hampering performance. We introduce a new unified VLA framework
for long-horizon tasks, dubbed LoHoVLA, to overcome these limitations. LoHoVLA
leverages a large pretrained vision language model (VLM) as the backbone to
jointly generate language and action tokens for sub-task generation and robot
action prediction, respectively. This shared representation promotes better
generalization across tasks. Additionally, LoHoVLA embraces a hierarchical
closed-loop control mechanism to mitigate errors originating from both
high-level planning and low-level control. To train LoHoVLA, we introduce
LoHoSet, a dataset built on the Ravens simulator, containing 20 long-horizon
tasks, each with 1,000 expert demonstrations composed of visual observations,
linguistic goals, sub-tasks, and robot actions. Experimental results show that
LoHoVLA significantly surpasses both hierarchical and standard VLA approaches
on long-horizon embodied tasks in the Ravens simulator. These findings
underscore the promise of unified architectures for advancing generalizable
embodied intelligence.

keyInsights: Unified architectures with shared VLM backbones can effectively integrate planning and control while maintaining generalization capabilities across diverse tasks
Hierarchical closed-loop control reduces computational overhead by avoiding unnecessary sub-task re-planning for transient low-level execution errors
Two-stage training (first optimizing text loss for planning, then both text and action losses) is crucial for balancing abstract reasoning and concrete action generation
originalProblem: Existing VLA models fall short in effective planning and reasoning for long-horizon tasks, relying on implicit sub-task planning that lacks interpretability and reliability
Hierarchical architectures suffer from suboptimal coordination and limited generalization between distinct planning and control modules, leading to error propagation and communication inefficiencies
results: Achieves 85.1% average reward and 81.0% success rate on complex reasoning tasks versus 8.2% for best hierarchical baseline
Demonstrates strong generalization to unseen tasks while requiring fewer high-level planning steps compared to strategies that re-plan after every failure
Shows that dataset expansion beyond original 4 LoHoRavens tasks is essential for preventing overfitting and enabling generalization to new task configurations
solution: Develop a unified VLA framework that jointly predicts both linguistic sub-tasks and robot actions using a shared VLM backbone (PaliGemma-3B) with augmented language head
Implement hierarchical closed-loop control that intelligently distinguishes between planning errors and execution errors, re-planning sub-tasks only when consecutive failures exceed threshold K=2
Create LoHoSet synthetic dataset with 20 long-horizon tasks and explicit sub-task annotations to enable proper training of the unified model
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Fudan University, ShanghaiTech University, and Shanghai Jiao Tong University develop LoHoVLA, a unified Vision-Language-Action model that integrates high-level task planning and low-level motion control within a single framework, achieving 85.1% success on complex reasoning tasks like "put-even-blocks-in-same-color-zone" versus 8.2% for hierarchical baselines through joint prediction of linguistic sub-tasks and discrete action tokens using an augmented PaliGemma-3B backbone, while introducing a hierarchical closed-loop control mechanism that triggers sub-task re-planning only after consecutive failures exceed a threshold (K=2), thereby reducing redundant high-level planning steps, and creating LoHoSet, a synthetic dataset of 20 diverse long-horizon tasks with explicit sub-task annotations that enables two-stage training for optimal performance across both planning and control capabilities.

title: LoHoVLA: A Unified Vision-Language-Action Model for Long-Horizon  Embodied Tasks

abstract:
Decision-making in complex, continuous multi-task environments is often
hindered by the difficulty of obtaining accurate models for planning and the
inefficiency of learning purely from trial and error. While precise environment
dynamics may be hard to specify, human experts can often provide high-fidelity
abstractions that capture the essential high-level structure of a task and user
preferences in the target environment. Existing hierarchical approaches often
target discrete settings and do not generalize across tasks. We propose a
hierarchical reinforcement learning approach that addresses these limitations
by dynamically planning over the expert-specified abstraction to generate
subgoals to learn a goal-conditioned policy. To overcome the challenges of
learning under sparse rewards, we shape the reward based on the optimal state
value in the abstract model. This structured decision-making process enhances
sample efficiency and facilitates zero-shot generalization. Our empirical
evaluation on a suite of procedurally generated continuous control environments
demonstrates that our approach outperforms existing hierarchical reinforcement
learning methods in terms of sample efficiency, task completion rate,
scalability to complex tasks, and generalization to novel scenarios.

keyInsights: Human experts can provide high-fidelity, high-level abstractions that capture the essential structure of complex continuous control tasks, serving as structured prior knowledge.
Even approximate expert knowledge, when integrated via abstractions, can significantly enhance reinforcement learning, particularly in sparse reward environments.
A hierarchical approach allows agents to reason at different levels of abstraction, with high-level planning in abstract space guiding low-level policy learning in continuous environments.
originalProblem: Reinforcement learning (RL) often suffers from high sample complexity in complex continuous control tasks.
Traditional RL methods struggle with sparse rewards, making exploration challenging.
RL agents typically exhibit limited generalization capabilities to new tasks or environments not seen during training.
results: GCRS consistently achieved superior sample efficiency, learning effective policies with fewer training samples compared to baselines across environments like DoorKey and MultiGoal.

The framework demonstrated strong zero-shot generalization, successfully completing tasks in new, unseen environments (e.g., MultiGoal with different wall counts and ObjectDelivery with new object colors) without additional training.

GCRS achieved approximately 95% success rate in MultiGoal environments, outperforming the next best method (Plan-RS with Grid abstraction) which reached about 50%, and maintained high performance as environment complexity increased.
solution: Researchers at Oregon State University propose Goal-Conditioned Reward Shaping (GCRS), a hierarchical reinforcement learning framework that integrates expert-defined abstractions into the learning process.
GCRS utilizes expert-defined abstraction functions (e.g., Grid, Room) and an abstract model to enable dynamic planning over abstract states to generate subgoals.
It employs potential-based reward shaping, derived from the abstract model's value function, to guide the learning of a goal-conditioned policy in the continuous environment.
paper_summary.summary: Oregon State University researchers introduce Goal-Conditioned Reward Shaping (GCRS), a hierarchical reinforcement learning framework that incorporates human expert-defined abstractions to improve learning efficiency and enable zero-shot generalization across continuous control tasks. GCRS demonstrates superior sample efficiency and achieves high success rates (e.g., 95% in MultiGoal) while effectively generalizing to new environments and object variations.

title: Learning with Expert Abstractions for Efficient Multi-Task Continuous  Control

abstract: Enabling humanoid robots to exploit physical contact, rather than simply avoid collisions, is crucial for autonomy in unstructured environments. Traditional optimization-based planners struggle with contact complexity, while on-policy reinforcement learning (RL) is sample-inefficient and has limited multi-task ability. We propose a framework combining a learned world model with sampling-based Model Predictive Control (MPC), trained on a demonstration-free offline dataset to predict future outcomes in a compressed latent space. To address sparse contact rewards and sensor noise, the MPC uses a learned surrogate value function for dense, robust planning. Our single, scalable model supports contact-aware tasks, including wall support after perturbation, blocking incoming objects, and traversing height-limited arches, with improved sample efficiency and multi-task capability over on-policy RL. Deployed on a physical humanoid, our system achieves robust, real-time contact planning from proprioception and ego-centric depth images. Code and dataset are available at our website: this https URL

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

keyInsights: Training a visual world model on a demonstration-free offline dataset is sufficient for learning complex contact dynamics, leading to significantly enhanced data efficiency compared to online reinforcement learning methods.

Utilizing a learned surrogate value function within a sampling-based Model Predictive Control (MPC) provides robust planning signals, effectively guiding the robot in environments characterized by sparse rewards and sensor noise.
An N-step surrogate optimization objective within MPC can reduce the variance of value estimates, enhancing planning stability and performance even when using an imperfect learned value function.
originalProblem: Humanoid robots face challenges in purposefully engaging in physical contact with their environment, often limited to collision avoidance rather than active interaction.

Existing contact planning methods, including traditional optimization-based and on-policy reinforcement learning, suffer from computational intensity, model inaccuracy sensitivity, sample inefficiency, and limited multi-task generalization.
Developing robust vision-based contact planning is difficult due to partial observability, sensor noise, and sparse rewards in real-world scenarios.
results: The framework achieved robust real-time performance on a physical Unitree G1 humanoid robot, successfully executing tasks like wall bracing, object blocking, and arch traversal at 25 Hz using ego-centric depth images.

It demonstrated superior sample efficiency, requiring approximately 0.5M data steps, significantly outperforming online reinforcement learning methods in complex visual tasks such as 'Traverse the Arch'.

A single multi-task model exhibited comparable or improved performance across three distinct contact tasks and generalized to out-of-distribution scenarios, such as blocking a previously unseen object.
solution: A scalable ego-vision world model was developed, trained solely on a demonstration-free offline dataset, to predict future outcomes in a compressed latent space rather than raw pixel data.
A value-guided sampling Model Predictive Control (MPC) framework was introduced, utilizing a learned surrogate value function as a heuristic for robust planning from pixel-based observations.
The framework integrates the world model with MPC to enable real-time, reactive contact planning on a physical humanoid robot using ego-centric depth images and proprioceptive feedback.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from the University of Michigan, UC Berkeley, and The Chinese University of Hong Kong developed an ego-vision world model combined with value-guided sampling Model Predictive Control, enabling humanoid robots to perform diverse physical contact tasks using only ego-centric depth images. This approach improves data efficiency and demonstrates robust real-time performance on a physical robot across multiple contact-rich scenarios.

title: Ego-Vision World Model for Humanoid Contact Planning

abstract: Long-horizon embodied planning is challenging because the world does not only change through an agent's actions: exogenous processes (e.g., water heating, dominoes cascading) unfold concurrently with the agent's actions. We propose a framework for abstract world models that jointly learns (i) symbolic state representations and (ii) causal processes for both endogenous actions and exogenous mechanisms. Each causal process models the time course of a stochastic cause-effect relation. We learn these world models from limited data via variational Bayesian inference combined with LLM proposals. Across five simulated tabletop robotics environments, the learned models enable fast planning that generalizes to held-out tasks with more objects and more complex goals, outperforming a range of baselines.

keyInsights: Explicitly modeling the stochastic temporal dynamics of both agent-initiated (endogenous) and autonomously unfolding (exogenous) causal processes is essential for robust and generalizable long-horizon robot planning.

Combining large foundation models (VLMs for predicate invention, LLMs for causal structure proposals) with efficient Bayesian inference provides a powerful mechanism for learning complex symbolic world models from limited demonstrations.

A "big-step" transition function, derived from learned causal processes, significantly enhances planning efficiency by allowing the planner to advance directly between abstract state changes, abstracting away fine-grained temporal details.
originalProblem: Long-horizon robot planning in dynamic environments is difficult due to concurrent, autonomous exogenous processes that change the world state independently of the agent.
Current abstract world models primarily assume instantaneous, agent-initiated actions, lacking the ability to explicitly model and abstract the time course of external environmental dynamics.
Learning symbolic planning models for environments with continuous or concurrent changes presents challenges such as combinatorial complexity and limited generalization in existing approaches.
results: ExoPredicator demonstrated superior performance, achieving near-perfect solve rates across five simulated robotics environments, consistently outperforming state-of-the-art HRL, VLM planning, and STRIPS-style operator learning baselines.

The framework achieved rapid model convergence within 1-3 online interaction iterations from limited demonstrations, effectively learning exogenous processes and outperforming manually engineered abstractions in several domains.
Bayesian model selection, guided by LLMs for proposing plausible conditions, proved crucial for efficiently and robustly learning the structure of causal processes from observational data.
solution: Develops ExoPredicator, a framework that learns abstract models of dynamic worlds by explicitly integrating symbolic state representation with causal processes, modeling both endogenous actions and autonomous exogenous dynamics with stochastic temporal delays.

Utilizes vision-language models (VLMs) for state abstraction, synthesizing Boolean predicates from perceptual input, and employs an online learning loop for efficient Bayesian inference to learn causal process parameters and structures.

Implements a big-step transition function and an A* planning algorithm that leverages the learned causal processes to reason about concurrent and delayed effects, enabling efficient long-horizon planning.
paper_summary.summary: A framework for robot planning learns abstract models of dynamic worlds, explicitly accounting for agent-initiated and autonomous causal processes with stochastic temporal delays. It achieves superior performance and near-perfect solve rates in simulated robotics tasks, outperforming hierarchical reinforcement learning and VLM planning baselines through effective integration of symbolic state abstraction and causal process modeling.

title: ExoPredicator: Learning Abstract Models of Dynamic Worlds for Robot Planning

abstract: World models are essential for autonomous robotic planning. However, the substantial computational overhead of existing dense Transformerbased models significantly hinders real-time deployment. To address this efficiency-performance bottleneck, we introduce DDP-WM, a novel world model centered on the principle of Disentangled Dynamics Prediction (DDP). We hypothesize that latent state evolution in observed scenes is heterogeneous and can be decomposed into sparse primary dynamics driven by physical interactions and secondary context-driven background updates. DDP-WM realizes this decomposition through an architecture that integrates efficient historical processing with dynamic localization to isolate primary dynamics. By employing a crossattention mechanism for background updates, the framework optimizes resource allocation and provides a smooth optimization landscape for planners. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DDP-WM achieves significant efficiency and performance across diverse tasks, including navigation, precise tabletop manipulation, and complex deformable or multi-body interactions. Specifically, on the challenging Push-T task, DDP-WM achieves an approximately 9 times inference speedup and improves the MPC success rate from 90% to98% compared to state-of-the-art dense models. The results establish a promising path for developing efficient, high-fidelity world models. Codes is available at this https URL.
title: DDP-WM: Disentangled Dynamics Prediction for Efficient World Models

abstract:
This paper introduces Hierarchical Diffusion Policy (HDP), a hierarchical
agent for multi-task robotic manipulation. HDP factorises a manipulation policy
into a hierarchical structure: a high-level task-planning agent which predicts
a distant next-best end-effector pose (NBP), and a low-level goal-conditioned
diffusion policy which generates optimal motion trajectories. The factorised
policy representation allows HDP to tackle both long-horizon task planning
while generating fine-grained low-level actions. To generate context-aware
motion trajectories while satisfying robot kinematics constraints, we present a
novel kinematics-aware goal-conditioned control agent, Robot Kinematics
Diffuser (RK-Diffuser). Specifically, RK-Diffuser learns to generate both the
end-effector pose and joint position trajectories, and distill the accurate but
kinematics-unaware end-effector pose diffuser to the kinematics-aware but less
accurate joint position diffuser via differentiable kinematics. Empirically, we
show that HDP achieves a significantly higher success rate than the
state-of-the-art methods in both simulation and real-world.

keyInsights: Combining a high-level next-best pose agent with a low-level, kinematics-aware diffusion model leverages the strengths of both end-to-end learning (context awareness) and modular approaches (sample efficiency).

The Robot Kinematics Diffuser (RK-Diffuser) effectively generates kinematically feasible joint trajectories by distilling information from a pose diffusion model using differentiable forward kinematics.

Differentiable kinematics is crucial for enabling the joint position diffusion model to learn accurate, contextually relevant trajectories from predicted end-effector poses, overcoming limitations of traditional inverse kinematics solvers.
originalProblem: Robotic manipulation of complex, long-horizon tasks in unstructured environments often faces a trade-off between flexible end-to-end learning (low sample efficiency) and efficient modular approaches (lack of context awareness).

Generating kinematically feasible robot trajectories while also respecting task context is challenging, as traditional motion planners like RRT can lack context awareness and inverse kinematics solvers can be error-prone.
Training complex robot manipulation policies typically requires large amounts of data, leading to low sample efficiency for real-world deployment.
results: The Hierarchical Diffusion Policy (HDP) achieved higher success rates than state-of-the-art methods on various challenging RLBench tasks.
The Robot Kinematics Diffuser (RK-Diffuser) consistently outperformed baseline diffusion policies and traditional motion planners in goal-conditioned motion generation.
HDP successfully demonstrated real-world applicability by training on only 20 demonstrations for an oven opening task, achieving a high success rate on a physical robot.
solution: A Hierarchical Diffusion Policy (HDP) is proposed, consisting of a high-level agent that predicts the next-best end-effector pose and a low-level Robot Kinematics Diffuser (RK-Diffuser) that generates the detailed motion trajectory.
The high-level agent utilizes a Transformer-based PerAct architecture with RGB-D observations and language instructions to determine goal poses.

The RK-Diffuser employs two interconnected diffusion models (one for end-effector poses, one for joint positions) and differentiable forward kinematics to generate kinematically feasible joint trajectories that satisfy the predicted pose goals.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from the Dyson Robot Learning Lab introduce the Hierarchical Diffusion Policy (HDP), a system for robotic manipulation that combines a high-level next-best pose prediction agent with a low-level, kinematics-aware diffusion model for motion generation. This approach achieves higher success rates on complex manipulation tasks in the RLBench benchmark and demonstrates real-world applicability with successful deployment on a physical robot using limited demonstrations.

title:
Hierarchical Diffusion Policy for Kinematics-Aware Multi-Task Robotic
  Manipulation

abstract: Diffusion-based generative methods have proven effective in modeling trajectories with offline datasets. However, they often face computational challenges and can falter in generalization, especially in capturing temporal abstractions for long-horizon tasks. To overcome this, we introduce the Hierarchical Diffuser, a simple, fast, yet surprisingly effective planning method combining the advantages of hierarchical and diffusion-based planning. Our model adopts a "jumpy" planning strategy at the higher level, which allows it to have a larger receptive field but at a lower computational cost -- a crucial factor for diffusion-based planning methods, as we have empirically verified. Additionally, the jumpy sub-goals guide our low-level planner, facilitating a fine-tuning stage and further improving our approach's effectiveness. We conducted empirical evaluations on standard offline reinforcement learning benchmarks, demonstrating our method's superior performance and efficiency in terms of training and planning speed compared to the non-hierarchical Diffuser as well as other hierarchical planning methods. Moreover, we explore our model's generalization capability, particularly on how our method improves generalization capabilities on compositional out-of-distribution tasks.

author_info.institution: Korea Advanced Institute of Science & Technology

keyInsights: Decomposing complex tasks into sub-goals and using a 'jumpy' planning strategy with a Sparse Diffuser significantly improves performance even without low-level refinement.
The hierarchical approach effectively increases the planner's receptive field, allowing it to capture longer-range dependencies without a linear increase in computational requirements.
Hierarchical decomposition acts as a regularization method, constraining the trajectory space and reducing overfitting, which aids in generalization.
originalProblem: Long-horizon planning in reinforcement learning faces exponential growth of action space, making efficient exploration and planning difficult.
Existing diffusion-based planners, like Diffuser, are computationally expensive for long horizons and struggle with generalization to compositional out-of-distribution (OOD) tasks.
Determining which actions contributed to success is challenging in tasks with sparse rewards and extended time horizons (credit assignment problem).
results: Hierarchical Diffuser (HD) outperforms previous methods on standard offline reinforcement learning benchmarks including Maze2D, AntMaze, Gym-MuJoCo, and FrankaKitchen.

HD demonstrates superior generalization to compositional out-of-distribution tasks, generating clear and direct trajectories for previously unseen start-goal pairs, unlike non-hierarchical diffusion methods.
HD exhibits faster training and planning compared to non-hierarchical diffusion methods, while also producing trajectories with better coverage of the dataset distribution.
solution: The Hierarchical Diffuser (HD) introduces a two-level planning hierarchy: a high-level "Sparse Diffuser" generates sparse sub-goals, and a low-level diffusion model plans detailed actions between these sub-goals.
The Sparse Diffuser is trained by subsampling trajectories from the dataset to generate sequences of states spaced several steps apart.
A second diffusion model, the Low-Level Diffuser, is trained to plan the detailed actions required to move from one sub-goal to the next, effectively connecting the sparse sub-goals.
paper_summary.summary: Chen et al. introduce the Hierarchical Diffuser (HD), a novel approach that combines hierarchical planning with diffusion models to address long-horizon planning challenges. HD achieves superior performance and computational efficiency on various offline RL benchmarks while also demonstrating improved generalization to out-of-distribution tasks.

title: Simple Hierarchical Planning with Diffusion

abstract: The ability to plan with temporal abstractions is central to intelligent decision-making. Rather than reasoning over primitive actions, we study agents that compose pre-trained policies as temporally extended actions, enabling solutions to complex tasks that no constituent alone can solve. Such compositional planning remains elusive as compounding errors in long-horizon predictions make it challenging to estimate the visitation distribution induced by sequencing policies. Motivated by the geometric policy composition framework introduced in arXiv:2206.08736, we address these challenges by learning predictive models of multi-step dynamics -- so-called jumpy world models -- that capture state occupancies induced by pre-trained policies across multiple timescales in an off-policy manner. Building on Temporal Difference Flows (arXiv:2503.09817), we enhance these models with a novel consistency objective that aligns predictions across timescales, improving long-horizon predictive accuracy. We further demonstrate how to combine these generative predictions to estimate the value of executing arbitrary sequences of policies over varying timescales. Empirically, we find that compositional planning with jumpy world models significantly improves zero-shot performance across a wide range of base policies on challenging manipulation and navigation tasks, yielding, on average, a 200% relative improvement over planning with primitive actions on long-horizon tasks.

keyInsights: Pre-trained policies can be treated as 'composable primitives' for flexible planning, extending their utility beyond individual task-specific performance.
Accurate prediction of multi-timescale future state occupancies is fundamental for effective compositional planning, mitigating compounding errors inherent in long-horizon predictions.
Enforcing consistency between predictions across different timescales is crucial for robustly learning world models that can reliably support long-horizon planning.
originalProblem: Existing 'foundation policies' often struggle with complex, long-horizon problems requiring intricate sequences of decisions.
Traditional model-based planning suffers from compounding errors when making long-horizon predictions based on primitive actions.
Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning (HRL) typically requires task-specific training, limiting zero-shot generalization to novel, complex tasks.
results: CompPlan achieved a 200% relative improvement over planning with primitive actions on long-horizon OGBench tasks (e.g., antmaze-giant, cube-4), significantly boosting zero-shot success rates.

The method substantially outperformed action-level planning (ActionPlan) by 201% and Generalized Policy Improvement (GPI) by 89%, and surpassed state-of-the-art hierarchical RL baselines like HIQL and SHARSA on challenging tasks.

The Temporal Difference Horizon Consistency (TD-HC) objective systematically improved the generative accuracy of GHMs, leading to a 28% reduction in Earth Mover's Distance for GC-1S policies in antmaze-giant.
solution: Learns 'jumpy world models,' specifically Geometric Horizon Models (GHMs), which predict multi-step state occupancies induced by pre-trained policies across multiple timescales.

Introduces a Temporal Difference Horizon Consistency (TD-HC) objective to accurately train GHMs by enforcing coherence between predictions at different timescales, enhancing long-horizon predictive accuracy.
Utilizes a Compositional Planning (CompPlan) framework to optimize sequences of pre-trained policies for novel tasks, enabling planning over temporal abstractions without task-specific training.
paper_summary.summary: FAIR at Meta and Mila researchers introduced Compositional Planning, a method for agents to solve complex, long-horizon tasks by dynamically composing pre-trained policies. This approach leverages multi-timescale 'jumpy world models' and shows a 200% relative improvement over planning with primitive actions on long-horizon OGBench benchmarks.

title: Compositional Planning with Jumpy World Models

abstract:
Deformable object manipulation stands as one of the most captivating yet
formidable challenges in robotics. While previous techniques have predominantly
relied on learning latent dynamics through demonstrations, typically
represented as either particles or images, there exists a pertinent limitation:
acquiring suitable demonstrations, especially for long-horizon tasks, can be
elusive. Moreover, basing learning entirely on demonstrations can hamper the
model's ability to generalize beyond the demonstrated tasks. In this work, we
introduce a demonstration-free hierarchical planning approach capable of
tackling intricate long-horizon tasks without necessitating any training. We
employ large language models (LLMs) to articulate a high-level, stage-by-stage
plan corresponding to a specified task. For every individual stage, the LLM
provides both the tool's name and the Python code to craft intermediate subgoal
point clouds. With the tool and subgoal for a particular stage at our disposal,
we present a granular closed-loop model predictive control strategy. This
leverages Differentiable Physics with Point-to-Point correspondence
(DiffPhysics-P2P) loss in the earth mover distance (EMD) space, applied
iteratively. Experimental findings affirm that our technique surpasses multiple
benchmarks in dough manipulation, spanning both short and long horizons.
Remarkably, our model demonstrates robust generalization capabilities to novel
and previously unencountered complex tasks without any preliminary
demonstrations. We further substantiate our approach with experimental trials
on real-world robotic platforms. Our project page:
this https URL
title: Make a Donut: Hierarchical EMD-Space Planning for Zero-Shot Deformable  Manipulation with Tools

abstract:
Current robotic planning methods often rely on predicting multi-frame images
with full pixel details. While this fine-grained approach can serve as a
generic world model, it introduces two significant challenges for downstream
policy learning: substantial computational costs that hinder real-time
deployment, and accumulated inaccuracies that can mislead action extraction.
Planning with coarse-grained subgoals partially alleviates efficiency issues.
However, their forward planning schemes can still result in off-task
predictions due to accumulation errors, leading to misalignment with long-term
goals. This raises a critical question: Can robotic planning be both efficient
and accurate enough for real-time control in long-horizon, multi-stage tasks?
To address this, we propose a Latent Space Backward Planning scheme (LBP),
which begins by grounding the task into final latent goals, followed by
recursively predicting intermediate subgoals closer to the current state. The
grounded final goal enables backward subgoal planning to always remain aware of
task completion, facilitating on-task prediction along the entire planning
horizon. The subgoal-conditioned policy incorporates a learnable token to
summarize the subgoal sequences and determines how each subgoal guides action
extraction. Through extensive simulation and real-robot long-horizon
experiments, we show that LBP outperforms existing fine-grained and forward
planning methods, achieving SOTA performance. Project Page:
this https URL

organization_info.name: Beijing University of Posts and Telecommunications

keyInsights: Backward planning inherently mitigates compounding errors by anchoring all generated subgoals to the ultimate task objective, ensuring consistent 'on-task' alignment.
Operating in a lightweight latent visual space for subgoal prediction significantly improves computational efficiency compared to pixel-level generative models.
A unified, self-supervised recursive subgoal predictor enhances robustness against prediction errors during test-time inference by learning to predict from its own previous outputs.
An adaptive goal-fusion module effectively processes diverse multi-horizon guidance from predicted subgoals and the final goal, allowing the policy to dynamically focus on relevant information.
originalProblem: Robotic planning for long-horizon, multi-stage tasks struggles with a 'fundamental trilemma' balancing computational efficiency, long-term accuracy, and consistent, on-task guidance.
Existing fine-grained (video planning) methods are computationally expensive and prone to compounding inaccuracies due to pixel-level prediction over long horizons.

Coarse-grained (subgoal planning) methods, while more efficient, still suffer from 'off-task predictions' and inconsistent long-term guidance due to error accumulation in their forward planning paradigm.
results: Achieved state-of-the-art performance on the LIBERO-LONG simulation benchmark, with average success rates up to 88.6% using DecisionNCE latent space, outperforming baselines by a large margin.
Demonstrated superior and consistent performance in challenging real-world AIRBOT tasks, maintaining high success rates through later stages where other methods experienced significant drops.

Showed consistently low prediction error across the entire planning horizon, directly demonstrating its effectiveness in mitigating error accumulation compared to traditional forward planning approaches.
Ablation studies confirmed the crucial contributions of the LBP planner, the latent visual goal, recursive subgoal prediction, and the Perceiver-style goal-fusion module to overall performance.
solution: LBP grounds the task objective by dynamically predicting a latent visual final goal from the current image observation and language instruction, providing a rich, context-dependent target.
A recursive 'coarse-to-fine' backward planning scheme generates intermediate subgoals in latent space, starting from the predicted final goal and moving towards the current state.
A context-conditioned policy uses a Perceiver-style cross-attention goal-fusion module to compress and adaptively leverage multi-horizon subgoal and goal information for robust action generation.
paper_summary.summary: The paper introduces Latent Space Backward Planning (LBP), a framework that learns robotic policies for long-horizon, multi-stage tasks by predicting subgoals in reverse temporal order within a latent visual space. LBP achieves state-of-the-art success rates of up to 88.6% on the LIBERO-LONG benchmark and consistently outperforms forward planning methods in real-world scenarios by mitigating compounding errors.

title: Efficient Robotic Policy Learning via Latent Space Backward Planning

abstract:
In this study, we address the challenge of learning generalizable policies
for compositional tasks defined by logical specifications. These tasks consist
of multiple temporally extended sub-tasks. Due to the sub-task
inter-dependencies and sparse reward issue in long-horizon tasks, existing
reinforcement learning (RL) approaches, such as task-conditioned and
goal-conditioned policies, continue to struggle with slow convergence and
sub-optimal performance in generalizing to compositional tasks. To overcome
these limitations, we introduce a new hierarchical RL framework that enhances
the efficiency and optimality of task generalization. At the high level, we
present an implicit planner specifically designed for generalizing
compositional tasks. This planner selects the next sub-task and estimates the
multi-step return for completing the remaining task to complete from the
current state. It learns a latent transition model and performs planning in the
latent space by using a graph neural network (GNN). Subsequently, the
high-level planner's selected sub-task guides the low-level agent to
effectively handle long-horizon tasks, while the multi-step return encourages
the low-level policy to account for future sub-task dependencies, enhancing its
optimality. We conduct comprehensive experiments to demonstrate the framework's
advantages over previous methods in terms of both efficiency and optimality.
title: Generalization of Compositional Tasks with Logical Specification via  Implicit Planning

abstract:
While modern policy optimization methods can do complex manipulation from
sensory data, they struggle on problems with extended time horizons and
multiple sub-goals. On the other hand, task and motion planning (TAMP) methods
scale to long horizons but they are computationally expensive and need to
precisely track world state. We propose a method that draws on the strength of
both methods: we train a policy to imitate a TAMP solver's output. This
produces a feed-forward policy that can accomplish multi-step tasks from
sensory data. First, we build an asynchronous distributed TAMP solver that can
produce supervision data fast enough for imitation learning. Then, we propose a
hierarchical policy architecture that lets us use partially trained control
policies to speed up the TAMP solver. In robotic manipulation tasks with 7-DoF
joint control, the partially trained policies reduce the time needed for
planning by a factor of up to 2.6. Among these tasks, we can learn a policy
that solves the RoboSuite 4-object pick-place task 88% of the time from object
pose observations and a policy that solves the RoboDesk 9-goal benchmark 79% of
the time from RGB images (averaged across the 9 disparate tasks).
title: Guided Imitation of Task and Motion Planning

abstract: To make effective decisions in novel environments with long-horizon goals, it is crucial to engage in hierarchical reasoning across spatial and temporal scales. This entails planning abstract subgoal sequences, visually reasoning about the underlying plans, and executing actions in accordance with the devised plan through visual-motor control. We propose Compositional Foundation Models for Hierarchical Planning (HiP), a foundation model which leverages multiple expert foundation model trained on language, vision and action data individually jointly together to solve long-horizon tasks. We use a large language model to construct symbolic plans that are grounded in the environment through a large video diffusion model. Generated video plans are then grounded to visual-motor control, through an inverse dynamics model that infers actions from generated videos. To enable effective reasoning within this hierarchy, we enforce consistency between the models via iterative refinement. We illustrate the efficacy and adaptability of our approach in three different long-horizon table-top manipulation tasks.

keyInsights: A compositional approach, integrating independently trained expert foundation models, offers a scalable and data-efficient alternative to monolithic models for complex robotic tasks.

Iterative refinement between hierarchical planning levels is crucial for maintaining consistency and feasibility across abstract goals, visual plans, and executable actions, preventing 'hallucinations' or unexecutable plans.
Pre-training modality-specific models on broad internet-scale data significantly reduces the reliance on expensive, task-specific robotics datasets, enhancing generalization capabilities.
originalProblem: Developing AI agents, particularly robots, capable of performing long-horizon decision-making tasks in novel environments that necessitate hierarchical reasoning.
The inherent scarcity and high cost associated with collecting large, diverse, and perfectly paired multi-modal (visual, language, action) datasets for robotic learning.
Limitations of existing monolithic foundation models or heavily fine-tuned large language models (LLMs) in robotics due to data expense and difficulties in scaling.
results: HiP significantly outperformed all baselines, including goal-conditioned policies and other planners, on various long-horizon table-top manipulation tasks, and demonstrated strong combinatorial generalization to unseen task variations.

Pre-training the video diffusion model on Ego4D and initializing the inverse dynamics model with VC-1 weights led to higher success rates and drastically reduced data requirements for fine-tuning (e.g., 10x less data for inverse dynamics).
Ablation studies confirmed that the iterative refinement mechanisms, particularly the visual grounding of LLM-generated subgoals, are critical for HiP's performance and robust planning.
solution: A three-tier hierarchical planning system that factorizes decision-making into abstract task planning, geometric visual planning, and low-level action execution.

Leverages separately trained, modality-specific foundation models: LLMs for abstract subgoal generation, video diffusion models for physically plausible visual trajectory generation, and inverse dynamics models for inferring low-level actions.
Implements iterative refinement mechanisms, including learned classifiers, to ensure consistency and physical plausibility across the different hierarchical levels and disparate foundation models.
paper_summary.summary: HiP (Compositional Foundation Models for Hierarchical Planning) introduces a robotic planning system that integrates distinct pre-trained foundation models for language, visual planning, and action control. It employs an iterative refinement mechanism to ensure consistency across these modalities, enabling robots to solve complex, long-horizon tasks in novel environments by leveraging broad pre-trained knowledge and reducing the need for extensive paired robotics data.

title: Compositional Foundation Models for Hierarchical Planning

abstract:
Long-horizon tasks, usually characterized by complex subtask dependencies,
present a significant challenge in manipulation planning. Skill chaining is a
practical approach to solving unseen tasks by combining learned skill priors.
However, such methods are myopic if sequenced greedily and face scalability
issues with search-based planning strategy. To address these challenges, we
introduce Generative Skill Chaining~(GSC), a probabilistic framework that
learns skill-centric diffusion models and composes their learned distributions
to generate long-horizon plans during inference. GSC samples from all skill
models in parallel to efficiently solve unseen tasks while enforcing geometric
constraints. We evaluate the method on various long-horizon tasks and
demonstrate its capability in reasoning about action dependencies, constraint
handling, and generalization, along with its ability to replan in the face of
perturbations. We show results in simulation and on real robot to validate the
efficiency and scalability of GSC, highlighting its potential for advancing
long-horizon task planning. More details are available at:
this https URL

keyInsights: Diffusion models can be adapted for compositional robotic planning by learning joint skill-level distributions and chaining their score functions to infer long-horizon sequences.
A dependency factor ($\\gamma=0.5$) for combining skill score functions is crucial for balancing forward and backward information flow, ensuring long-term consistency in plans.
Classifier-based guidance provides a flexible and powerful mechanism to incorporate arbitrary, test-time specified constraints into generative planning without model retraining.
originalProblem: Long-horizon manipulation planning faces challenges from complex inter-step dependencies and prohibitively large search spaces.
Existing methods (e.g., TAMP, discriminative skill chaining) struggle with generalization to unseen tasks, scalability for long sequences, and efficient constraint handling.
Prior generative planning approaches primarily focus on capturing distributions for known training tasks, lacking the ability to compose individual skills flexibly for novel, unseen tasks.
results: GSC achieved success rates comparable to or better than state-of-the-art search-based baselines (e.g., 0.68 vs 0.40 for STAP in "Rearrangement Push Task 1") on complex, unseen long-horizon tasks in simulation.
Demonstrated effective constraint handling, with GSC + guidance significantly improving success rates (e.g., 1.0 vs 0.50 for GSC without guidance) when imposing additional objectives.
Validated real-world applicability and robustness on a Franka Panda robot, successfully executing 8-skill sequences and enabling effective replanning in perturbed environments.
solution: GSC develops a probabilistic framework by training individual skill-level diffusion models to capture joint state-action-state distributions for primitive actions.

It formulates long-horizon planning as sampling from a joint distribution over an entire sequence, linearly chaining the score functions of individual skill diffusion models using a dependency factor (gamma).
Task-specific constraints are flexibly integrated at inference time using classifier-based guidance, adding a gradient term to the combined score function.
paper_summary.summary: Generative Skill Chaining (GSC) introduces a probabilistic framework that leverages skill-centric diffusion models for long-horizon robotic manipulation planning. This approach enables the generation of valid and robust skill sequences for unseen complex tasks, incorporating flexible constraint handling, and demonstrating higher success rates than search-based baselines in simulation, while also proving effective on a physical robot.

title: Generative Skill Chaining: Long-Horizon Skill Planning with Diffusion  Models

abstract:
Long-horizon manipulation tasks such as stacking represent a longstanding
challenge in the field of robotic manipulation, particularly when using
reinforcement learning (RL) methods which often struggle to learn the correct
sequence of actions for achieving these complex goals. To learn this sequence,
symbolic planning methods offer a good solution based on high-level reasoning,
however, planners often fall short in addressing the low-level control
specificity needed for precise execution. This paper introduces a novel
framework that integrates symbolic planning with hierarchical RL through the
cooperation of high-level operators and low-level policies. Our contribution
integrates planning operators (e.g. preconditions and effects) as part of the
hierarchical RL algorithm based on the Scheduled Auxiliary Control (SAC-X)
method. We developed a dual-purpose high-level operator, which can be used both
in holistic planning and as independent, reusable policies. Our approach offers
a flexible solution for long-horizon tasks, e.g., stacking a cube. The
experimental results show that our proposed method obtained an average of 97.2%
success rate for learning and executing the whole stack sequence, and the
success rate for learning independent policies, e.g. reach (98.9%), lift
(99.7%), stack (85%), etc. The training time is also reduced by 68% when using
our proposed approach.
title: Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning Based on Planning Operators

abstract: Model-based planning in robotic domains is fundamentally challenged by the hybrid nature of physical dynamics, where continuous motion is punctuated by discrete events such as contacts and impacts. Conventional latent world models typically employ monolithic neural networks that enforce global continuity, inevitably over-smoothing the distinct dynamic modes (e.g., sticking vs. sliding, flight vs. stance). For a planner, this smoothing results in catastrophic compounding errors during long-horizon lookaheads, rendering the search process unreliable at physical boundaries. To address this, we introduce the Prismatic World Model (PRISM-WM), a structured architecture designed to decompose complex hybrid dynamics into composable primitives. PRISM-WM leverages a context-aware Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) framework where a gating mechanism implicitly identifies the current physical mode, and specialized experts predict the associated transition dynamics. We further introduce a latent orthogonalization objective to ensure expert diversity, effectively preventing mode collapse. By accurately modeling the sharp mode transitions in system dynamics, PRISM-WM significantly reduces rollout drift. Extensive experiments on challenging continuous control benchmarks, including high-dimensional humanoids and diverse multi-task settings, demonstrate that PRISM-WM provides a superior high-fidelity substrate for trajectory optimization algorithms (e.g., TD-MPC), proving its potential as a powerful foundational model for next-generation model-based agents.

keyInsights: Hybrid dynamics can be effectively decomposed into specialized, orthogonal latent regimes, allowing the model to capture sharp mode transitions more accurately than monolithic approaches.
Enforcing structural non-redundancy among dynamics experts (via orthogonalization) is more effective for achieving precise dynamics fidelity than simply increasing model capacity.

The structured world model spontaneously recovers quasi-discrete dynamics, learning causal and state-dependent decompositions that mirror real-world physical transitions, rather than over-smoothing them.
originalProblem: Monolithic neural networks in world models tend to 'over-smooth' distinct dynamic modes (e.g., contact vs. non-contact) inherent in hybrid robotic systems.

This over-smoothing leads to catastrophic compounding errors when models are unrolled over long planning horizons, severely limiting reliable lookaheads and robust decision-making in Model-Based Reinforcement Learning.
results: Achieved superior sample efficiency and asymptotic rewards across high-dimensional locomotion tasks (DiffRL) and complex humanoid control benchmarks, outperforming monolithic baselines.
Improved multi-task generalization, achieving a mean normalized score of 0.531 on the MT30 benchmark, a 23.5% improvement over TD-MPC2.

Extended the effective planning horizon from approximately H=5 (for monolithic models) to H=30 by drastically reducing latent dynamics and reward prediction mean squared error, maintaining trajectory plausibility.
solution: The Prismatic World Model (PRISM-WM) is a context-aware Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture designed to decompose complex hybrid dynamics into specialized, composable components.
A latent orthogonalization objective (using the Gram-Schmidt process) is introduced to ensure experts learn distinct, non-redundant dynamic primitives, preventing mode collapse.
PRISM-WM serves as a drop-in replacement for conventional world models, enhancing both online planning (e.g., TD-MPC) and direct policy learning (e.g., PWM) by providing higher-fidelity dynamics.
paper_summary.summary: PRISM-WM introduces a world model that leverages a Mixture-of-Experts architecture with latent orthogonalization to accurately model hybrid dynamics in robotic systems. This approach significantly extends reliable planning horizons and achieves superior performance in high-dimensional continuous control tasks.

title: Prismatic World Model: Learning Compositional Dynamics for Planning in Hybrid Systems

abstract: We present a visually grounded hierarchical planning algorithm for long-horizon manipulation tasks. Our algorithm offers a joint framework of neuro-symbolic task planning and low-level motion generation conditioned on the specified goal. At the core of our approach is a two-level scene graph representation, namely geometric scene graph and symbolic scene graph. This hierarchical representation serves as a structured, object-centric abstraction of manipulation scenes. Our model uses graph neural networks to process these scene graphs for predicting high-level task plans and low-level motions. We demonstrate that our method scales to long-horizon tasks and generalizes well to novel task goals. We validate our method in a kitchen storage task in both physical simulation and the real world. Our experiments show that our method achieved over 70% success rate and nearly 90% of subgoal completion rate on the real robot while being four orders of magnitude faster in computation time compared to standard search-based task-and-motion planner.

keyInsights: Integrating neuro-symbolic task planning with graph-based motion generation effectively bridges the gap between discrete symbolic reasoning and continuous robot control, enabling strong generalization and efficiency.

Hierarchical scene graphs provide a structured, object-centric abstraction that allows GNNs to leverage relational inductive biases, crucial for generalizing to varying scene compositions and novel tasks.

The Regression Planning Network's backward planning mechanism significantly improves performance for long-horizon tasks by recursively predicting previous subgoals, overcoming limitations of direct subgoal prediction.
originalProblem: Robots face challenges with long-horizon manipulation, requiring complex sequences of actions over extended periods in unstructured environments.
Existing methods, like Task and Motion Planning (TAMP), suffer from reliance on handcrafted rules, abstraction from raw perception, and high computational expense.
Data-driven deep learning approaches for planning struggle with generalization to novel or very long-horizon tasks, despite handling raw sensory input.
results: In simulation, the method achieved up to 0.67 success rate for 6-object tasks, significantly outperforming MLP (0.44 success) and a direct subgoal prediction baseline (0.00 success).

The neuro-symbolic task planner demonstrated a four orders of magnitude speedup in computation time compared to the state-of-the-art TAMP solver PDDLStream (e.g., 0.15s vs. 127.9s for 2-object tasks).
On a real Franka Emika Panda robot using raw RGB input, the approach achieved a 70.6% overall success rate and 89.6% subgoal completion rate across 34 trials, validating its practical robustness.
solution: A two-level scene graph representation is introduced: a low-level geometric graph captures 6-DoF object poses from RGB images, and a high-level symbolic graph describes semantic object states and relations.

A Neuro-Symbolic Task Planning module, based on a Regression Planning Network (RPN) with Graph Neural Networks (GNNs), performs backward planning in symbolic space to predict immediate subgoals without handcrafted rules.

A Graph-based Motion Generation module uses GNNs to ground symbolic subgoals to the geometric scene graph, predicting continuous parameters for motion primitives (pick, place, push) to execute low-level actions.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from NVIDIA and The University of Texas at Austin introduce a visually-grounded hierarchical planning framework that combines neuro-symbolic task planning with graph-based motion generation for complex, long-horizon manipulation tasks. This approach achieves a four orders of magnitude speedup in task planning over traditional methods and demonstrates robust performance with up to 70.6% overall success on a real robot operating directly from visual input.

title: Hierarchical Planning for Long-Horizon Manipulation with Geometric and Symbolic Scene Graphs

abstract: Learning robust robotic control policies remains a major challenge due to the high cost of collecting labeled data, limited generalization to unseen environments, and difficulties in planning over long horizons. While Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models offer a promising solution by grounding natural language instructions into single-step control commands, they often lack mechanisms for lookahead and struggle with compounding errors in dynamic tasks. In this project, we introduce Scaling Inference-Time COMpute for VLAs (SITCOM), a framework that augments any pretrained VLA with model-based rollouts and reward-based trajectory selection, inspired by Model Predictive Control algorithm. SITCOM leverages a learned dynamics model to simulate multi-step action rollouts to select the best candidate plan for real-world execution, transforming one-shot VLAs into robust long-horizon planners. We develop an efficient transformer-based dynamics model trained on large-scale BridgeV2 data and fine-tuned on SIMPLER environments to bridge the Real2Sim gap, and score candidate rollouts using rewards from simulator. Through comprehensive evaluation across multiple tasks and settings in the SIMPLER environment, we demonstrate that SITCOM when combined with a good reward function can significantly improve task completion rate from 48% to 72% using trained dynamics model.

keyInsights: Integrating model-based planning significantly transforms reactive, single-step VLAs into proactive, long-horizon planners, crucial for complex robotic tasks.
Domain adaptation through fine-tuning is essential for both the core VLA policy and the learned dynamics model to effectively bridge the "Real2Sim" gap and ensure practical performance.
