Scaling inference-time compute by increasing the breadth (number of candidate rollouts) and depth (length of rollouts) can yield continuous performance gains for challenging tasks, despite potential computational increases.
originalProblem: Existing Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models primarily operate in a "one-shot" manner, predicting only the immediate next action without explicit lookahead or planning capabilities.
This lack of foresight leads to compounding errors in dynamic environments and limits the ability of VLAs to execute complex, multi-step tasks requiring long-horizon planning.
Traditional robot control policies struggle with high data collection costs and poor generalization to novel environments.
results: SITCOM (World Model) achieved an average success rate of 72% in SIMPLER manipulation tasks, a significant improvement over the fine-tuned OpenVLA-SFT (48%) and the baseline OpenVLA (1%).

Fine-tuning the dynamics model on in-domain SIMPLER trajectories reduced its Frechet Inception Distance (FID) from 17.0 to 11.2 and Optical Flow Loss (OFL) from 1.665 to 0.992, indicating improved state prediction.

The DAgger-inspired training strategy for the dynamics model effectively improved reconstruction quality and reduced prediction drift over longer horizons compared to single-timestep prediction training.
solution: SITCOM introduces an inference-time planning framework that augments pre-trained VLAs with model-based rollouts and reward-ranking capabilities, inspired by Model Predictive Control.

An efficient transformer-based dynamics model is developed to simulate multi-step action sequences, pre-trained on BridgeV2 and fine-tuned on target environments (SIMPLER) with a DAgger-inspired adaptation strategy to mitigate error accumulation.

The framework samples candidate actions, simulates their future trajectories using the dynamics model, evaluates trajectories with a reward model, and executes only the first action of the highest-scoring plan in a receding horizon loop.
paper_summary.summary: The SITCOM framework from Carnegie Mellon University enhances Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models by integrating an inference-time model-based planning mechanism. This system leverages a learned transformer-based dynamics model for multi-step action rollouts, achieving an average success rate of 72% on various simulated robotic manipulation tasks and substantially outperforming reactive VLA baselines.

title: SITCOM: Scaling Inference-Time COMpute for VLAs

abstract: World models are becoming central to robotic planning and control as they enable prediction of future state transitions. Existing approaches often emphasize video generation or natural-language prediction, which are difficult to ground in robot actions and suffer from compounding errors over long horizons. Classic task and motion planning models world transitions in logical space, enabling robot-executable and robust long-horizon reasoning. However, they typically operate independently of visual perception, preventing synchronized symbolic and visual state prediction. We propose a Hierarchical World Model (H-WM) that jointly predicts logical and visual state transitions within a unified framework. H-WM combines a high-level logical world model with a low-level visual world model, integrating the long-horizon robustness of symbolic reasoning with visual grounding. The hierarchical outputs provide stable intermediate guidance for long-horizon tasks, mitigating error accumulation and enabling robust execution across extended task sequences. Experiments across multiple vision-language-action (VLA) control policies demonstrate the effectiveness and generality of H-WM's guidance.

keyInsights: Learning symbolic planning dynamics directly from data using large language models can provide robust, long-horizon logical guidance even with incomplete state labels.
Predicting latent visual features for end-of-subtask states offers more effective and compact visual guidance compared to pixel-level image generation, reducing error accumulation.
The joint and synchronized prediction of logical and visual state transitions within a hierarchical framework is crucial for robust VLA execution in long-horizon tasks.
originalProblem: End-to-end Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models show significant performance degradation on complex, long-horizon robotic tasks due to compounding errors and ambiguous goal specifications.

Current intermediate guidance methods, such as LLM-based planners and visual world models, suffer from issues like semantic-execution misalignment or compounding prediction errors over extended horizons.
Classical Task and Motion Planning (TAMP) offers long-horizon consistency but lacks direct visual grounding, leading to brittleness in unstructured environments.
results: H-WM-guided VLA policies achieved an average success rate of 64.8% and a Q-score of 84.9% on the challenging LIBERO-LoHo benchmark, significantly outperforming unguided methods (6.4% success, 55.3% Q-score).
Ablation studies showed that logical guidance alone improved success rate by over 40%, with an additional >17% gain from visual guidance, demonstrating the synergistic effect of both.
Real-world deployment on a UR5e robot for an 8-step table-cleaning task confirmed improved long-horizon success and more accurate pose generation when utilizing H-WM's guidance.
solution: Developed a Hierarchical World Model (H-WM) that provides multi-level guidance by jointly predicting logical and visual state transitions.
Incorporated a Logic World Model (M_L) that learns symbolic planning dynamics from data using a fine-tuned Large Language Model (LLM), performing long-horizon reasoning.
Designed a Visual World Model to predict stable latent visual features for end-of-subtask states, conditioned on future logical transitions, instead of generating pixel-level images.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Huawei Noah’s Ark Lab and affiliated universities developed H-WM, a Hierarchical World Model that guides robotic task and motion planning by jointly predicting logical and visual state transitions. This model substantially improves performance on long-horizon tasks, achieving an average success rate of 64.8% compared to 6.4% for unguided methods on the LIBERO-LoHo benchmark.

title: H-WM: Robotic Task and Motion Planning Guided by Hierarchical World Model

abstract:
Existing robot policies predominantly adopt the task-centric approach,
requiring end-to-end task data collection. This results in limited
generalization to new tasks and difficulties in pinpointing errors within
long-horizon, multi-stage tasks. To address this, we propose RoboMatrix, a
skill-centric hierarchical framework designed for scalable robot task planning
and execution in open-world environments. RoboMatrix extracts general
meta-skills from diverse complex tasks, enabling the completion of unseen tasks
through skill composition. Its architecture consists of a high-level scheduling
layer that utilizes large language models (LLMs) for task decomposition, an
intermediate skill layer housing meta-skill models, and a low-level hardware
layer for robot control. A key innovation of our work is the introduction of
the first unified vision-language-action (VLA) model capable of seamlessly
integrating both movement and manipulation within one model. This is achieved
by combining vision and language prompts to generate discrete actions.
Experimental results demonstrate that RoboMatrix achieves a 50% higher success
rate than task-centric baselines when applied to unseen objects, scenes, and
tasks. To advance open-world robotics research, we will open-source code,
hardware designs, model weights, and datasets at
this https URL

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

keyInsights: Decomposing complex tasks into generalizable meta-skills significantly improves robot generalization to novel objects, scenes, and task compositions, while reducing overall data requirements.
A unified VLA model can effectively learn and execute a broad range of discrete, atomic skills by leveraging multimodal alignment pre-training and supervised fine-tuning on fine-grained skill data.

A hierarchical architecture, with an LLM for task decomposition and an execution checker for validating subtask feasibility, enhances interpretability and robustness, crucial for deployment in dynamic, open-world settings.
originalProblem: Existing end-to-end robot policies, even with advanced Vision-Language Models (VLMs), struggle to generalize effectively to new tasks, unseen objects, or novel environmental configurations.
Traditional task-centric approaches require extensive data collection and re-training for each new complex task, leading to exponential data demands and poor scalability.
The 'black-box' nature of end-to-end learning makes it difficult to diagnose errors and pinpoint failures in long-horizon, multi-stage robotic tasks.
results: RoboMatrix achieved 90-100% success rates on meta-skills and outperformed task-centric baselines by up to 50% in Level V generalization scenarios involving novel objects, scenes, and task compositions.

The skill-centric method demonstrated an 80% success rate on hard-level long-horizon tasks (over 5 steps in unseen scenarios), a 40% improvement over task-centric methods, and showed robustness to dynamic human interferences.

Multimodal pre-training and domain-specific alignment significantly improved VLA model performance, boosting overall success on a sequential long-horizon task from 30% to 100%, with larger model sizes (13B vs. 7B) consistently yielding higher success rates.
solution: A skill-centric pipeline that defines a finite set of reusable 'meta-skills' and uses an iterative data engine for efficient, continuous collection and refinement of skill-specific training data.

A dual-model Skill Layer featuring a unified Vision-Language-Action (VLA) model that integrates movement and manipulation, based on a decode-only LLM (Vicuna 1.5) and a visual encoder (CLIP), alongside hybrid models for specific deterministic skills.

A hierarchical framework comprising a GPT-powered Modular Scheduling Layer for high-level task planning and execution checking, a Skill Layer for action generation, and a Hardware Layer for physical control and real-time state observation.
paper_summary.summary: RoboMatrix introduces a skill-centric hierarchical framework that enhances robot generalization, data efficiency, and robustness for task planning and execution in open-world environments. This approach decomposes complex tasks into reusable meta-skills, allowing robots to adapt to novel objects, scenes, and dynamic interactions.

title: RoboMatrix: A Skill-centric Hierarchical Framework for Scalable Robot  Task Planning and Execution in Open-World

abstract: Generalizable long-horizon robotic assembly requires reasoning at multiple levels of abstraction. While end-to-end imitation learning (IL) is a promising approach, it typically requires large amounts of expert demonstration data and often struggles to achieve the high precision demanded by assembly tasks. Reinforcement learning (RL) approaches, on the other hand, have shown some success in high-precision assembly, but suffer from sample inefficiency, which limits their effectiveness in long-horizon tasks. To address these challenges, we propose a hierarchical modular approach, named Adaptive Robotic Compositional Hierarchy (ARCH), which enables long-horizon, high-precision robotic assembly in contact-rich settings. ARCH employs a hierarchical planning framework, including a low-level primitive library of parameterized skills and a high-level policy. The low-level primitive library includes essential skills for assembly tasks, such as grasping and inserting. These primitives consist of both RL and model-based policies. The high-level policy, learned via IL from a handful of demonstrations, without the need for teleoperation, selects the appropriate primitive skills and instantiates them with input parameters. We extensively evaluate our approach in simulation and on a real robotic manipulation platform. We show that ARCH generalizes well to unseen objects and outperforms baseline methods in terms of success rate and data efficiency. More details are available at: this https URL.

keyInsights: Combining model-based motion planning with reinforcement learning for low-level primitives effectively handles both free-space movements and delicate contact-rich interactions.
A hierarchical structure allows a high-level policy to compose primitives from limited demonstrations, overcoming the data inefficiency of end-to-end learning for long-horizon tasks.
Abstracting continuous parameter inputs during data collection simplifies human demonstrations, enabling efficient learning without requiring direct teleoperation for precise control.
originalProblem: Automating complex, long-horizon robotic assembly tasks that involve delicate physical contact and demand high precision.
Existing end-to-end learning methods require vast amounts of data, while reinforcement learning is sample inefficient for long sequences.
Traditional task and motion planning (TAMP) relies on labor-intensive hand-designed components and struggles with dynamic environments.
results: Demonstrated a 55% success rate (SR) and 0.51 Success weighted by Path Length (SPL) on a real-world 9-object FMB assembly task, outperforming other hierarchical and end-to-end baselines.
Exhibited strong generalization capabilities by successfully assembling 8 novel objects, achieving up to 80% SR for some unseen parts without additional training.
Achieved high performance and robustness across tasks using only 10 human demonstrations for the high-level policy, showing high data efficiency.
solution: Developed ARCH, a hierarchical framework decomposing complex assembly into low-level primitives and a high-level orchestrating policy.
Implemented low-level primitives using a hybrid approach: motion planning for free-space movements and reinforcement learning for contact-rich operations like insertion.
Trained the high-level policy using imitation learning with a Diffusion Transformer from a small number of human demonstrations, where continuous parameters are automatically derived.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Stanford, MIT, University of Michigan, and Autodesk Research introduced ARCH, a hierarchical hybrid learning framework that enables robots to perform complex, multi-stage assembly tasks involving contact with high precision. The framework achieved a 55% success rate on a real-world assembly task with 9 objects using only 10 demonstrations and demonstrated generalization to 8 unseen objects.

title: ARCH: Hierarchical Hybrid Learning for Long-Horizon Contact-Rich Robotic Assembly

abstract: Learning predictive world models is crucial for enhancing the planning capabilities of reinforcement learning (RL) agents. Recently, MuZero-style algorithms, leveraging the value equivalence principle and Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS), have achieved superhuman performance in various domains. However, these methods struggle to scale in heterogeneous scenarios with diverse dependencies and task variability. To overcome these limitations, we introduce UniZero, a novel approach that employs a modular transformer-based world model to effectively learn a shared latent space. By concurrently predicting latent dynamics and decision-oriented quantities conditioned on the learned latent history, UniZero enables joint optimization of the long-horizon world model and policy, facilitating broader and more efficient planning in the latent space. We show that UniZero significantly outperforms existing baselines in benchmarks that require long-term memory. Additionally, UniZero demonstrates superior scalability in multitask learning experiments conducted on Atari benchmarks. In standard single-task RL settings, such as Atari and DMControl, UniZero matches or even surpasses the performance of current state-of-the-art methods. Finally, extensive ablation studies and visual analyses validate the effectiveness and scalability of UniZero's design choices. Our code is available at \textcolor{magenta}{this https URL}.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

title: UniZero: Generalized and Efficient Planning with Scalable Latent World Models

abstract: The framework of mixed observable Markov decision processes (MOMDP) models many robotic domains in which some state variables are fully observable while others are not. In this work, we identify a significant subclass of MOMDPs defined by how actions influence the fully observable components of the state and how those, in turn, influence the partially observable components and the rewards. This unique property allows for a two-level hierarchical approach we call HIerarchical Reinforcement Learning under Mixed Observability (HILMO), which restricts partial observability to the top level while the bottom level remains fully observable, enabling higher learning efficiency. The top level produces desired goals to be reached by the bottom level until the task is solved. We further develop theoretical guarantees to show that our approach can achieve optimal and quasi-optimal behavior under mild assumptions. Empirical results on long-horizon continuous control tasks demonstrate the efficacy and efficiency of our approach in terms of improved success rate, sample efficiency, and wall-clock training time. We also deploy policies learned in simulation on a real robot.
title: Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning under Mixed Observability

abstract: We propose a new approach for solving planning problems with a hierarchical structure, fusing reinforcement learning and MPC planning. Our formulation tightly and elegantly couples the two planning paradigms. It leverages reinforcement learning actions to inform the MPPI sampler, and adaptively aggregates MPPI samples to inform the value estimation. The resulting adaptive process leverages further MPPI exploration where value estimates are uncertain, and improves training robustness and the overall resulting policies. This results in a robust planning approach that can handle complex planning problems and easily adapts to different applications, as demonstrated over several domains, including race driving, modified Acrobot, and Lunar Lander with added obstacles. Our results in these domains show better data efficiency and overall performance in terms of both rewards and task success, with up to a 72% increase in success rate compared to existing approaches, as well as accelerated convergence (x2.1) compared to non-adaptive sampling.

keyInsights: Integrating MPC as a 'structured sampler' that generates virtual rollouts for RL training dramatically enhances sample efficiency and safe exploration compared to standalone RL methods.

A bi-directional information flow, where RL guides MPPI's objective and MPPI's diverse trajectories inform RL's value function and policy updates, creates a more robust and data-efficient learning system.

Dynamically adjusting the weight of virtual data based on value function uncertainty is critical for maintaining robustness in environments with model mismatch, allowing for faster convergence while minimizing asymptotic bias.
originalProblem: Traditional Reinforcement Learning (RL) methods are sample inefficient, demanding extensive, often costly or unsafe, real-world interactions for training.
Model Predictive Control (MPC) requires accurate system models and can struggle with long-horizon planning and insufficient exploration.
Existing hybrid RL-MPC approaches often lack a principled, bi-directional information flow, leading to limited performance and vulnerability to bias from model approximations.
results: The proposed framework consistently outperformed standard PPO and SAC baselines across Acrobot, Lunar Lander, and Racing environments, which frequently failed or exhibited conservative behaviors.
Utilizing virtual data mixing (PPO-MPPI with fixed ρ=0.3) led to superior performance, achieving up to a 72% increase in success rate on Lunar Lander compared to MPPI-guided PPO without virtual data.
The adaptive influence ratio was crucial in the Racing environment with highly mismatched models, achieving the highest rewards and converging 2.1 times faster than the best fixed mixing ratio.
solution: A two-layer hierarchical design couples a high-level value-based RL policy with a low-level Model Predictive Path Integral (MPPI) controller, where RL actions condition the MPPI objective for local optimal control.
MPPI generates 'virtual rollouts' from an approximate model, providing structured exploration data that augments the RL training buffer without costly real environment interactions.

An adaptive influence ratio dynamically mixes real and virtual datasets based on the estimated uncertainty of the value function, mitigating bias introduced by model approximations and ensuring robust learning.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at the Toyota Research Institute developed an Adaptive Hierarchical RL-MPC framework that tightly integrates a high-level reinforcement learning policy with a low-level Model Predictive Path Integral controller. This approach significantly improves sample efficiency and robustness in complex decision-making tasks by generating virtual rollouts from the MPC and adaptively balancing real and virtual data, leading to up to a 72% increase in success rate and 2.1 times faster convergence in highly mismatched model environments.

title: Learning to Plan, Planning to Learn: Adaptive Hierarchical RL-MPC for Sample-Efficient Decision Making

abstract: Decision-making is challenging in robotics environments with continuous object-centric states, continuous actions, long horizons, and sparse feedback. Hierarchical approaches, such as task and motion planning (TAMP), address these challenges by decomposing decision-making into two or more levels of abstraction. In a setting where demonstrations and symbolic predicates are given, prior work has shown how to learn symbolic operators and neural samplers for TAMP with manually designed parameterized policies. Our main contribution is a method for learning parameterized polices in combination with operators and samplers. These components are packaged into modular neuro-symbolic skills and sequenced together with search-then-sample TAMP to solve new tasks. In experiments in four robotics domains, we show that our approach -- bilevel planning with neuro-symbolic skills -- can solve a wide range of tasks with varying initial states, goals, and objects, outperforming six baselines and ablations. Video: this https URL Code: this https URL
title: Learning Neuro-Symbolic Skills for Bilevel Planning

abstract: Long-horizon planning for robot manipulation is a challenging problem that requires reasoning about the effects of a sequence of actions on a physical 3D scene. While traditional task planning methods are shown to be effective for long-horizon manipulation, they require discretizing the continuous state and action space into symbolic descriptions of objects, object relationships, and actions. Instead, we propose a hybrid learning-and-planning approach that leverages learned models as domain-specific priors to guide search in high-dimensional continuous action spaces. We introduce SPOT: Search over Point cloud Object Transformations, which plans by searching for a sequence of transformations from an initial scene point cloud to a goal-satisfying point cloud. SPOT samples candidate actions from learned suggesters that operate on partially observed point clouds, eliminating the need to discretize actions or object relationships. We evaluate SPOT on multi-object rearrangement tasks, reporting task planning success and task execution success in both simulation and real-world environments. Our experiments show that SPOT generates successful plans and outperforms a policy-learning approach. We also perform ablations that highlight the importance of search-based planning.
title: Planning from Point Clouds over Continuous Actions for Multi-object Rearrangement

abstract: Solving long-horizon, temporally-extended tasks using Reinforcement Learning (RL) is challenging, compounded by the common practice of learning without prior knowledge (or tabula rasa learning). Humans can generate and execute plans with temporally-extended actions and quickly learn to perform new tasks because we almost never solve problems from scratch. We want autonomous agents to have this same ability. Recently, LLMs have been shown to encode a tremendous amount of knowledge about the world and to perform impressive in-context learning and reasoning. However, using LLMs to solve real world problems is hard because they are not grounded in the current task. In this paper we exploit the planning capabilities of LLMs while using RL to provide learning from the environment, resulting in a hierarchical agent that uses LLMs to solve long-horizon tasks. Instead of completely relying on LLMs, they guide a high-level policy, making learning significantly more sample efficient. This approach is evaluated in simulation environments such as MiniGrid, SkillHack, and Crafter, and on a real robot arm in block manipulation tasks. We show that agents trained using our approach outperform other baselines methods and, once trained, don't need access to LLMs during deployment.
title: LLM Augmented Hierarchical Agents

abstract: Model Predictive Control (MPC) is among the most widely adopted and reliable methods for robot control, relying critically on an accurate dynamics model. However, existing dynamics models used in the gradient-based MPC are limited by computational complexity and state representation. To address this limitation, we propose the Hyper Prediction Model (HyperPM) - a novel approach in which we project the unmodeled dynamics onto a time-dependent dynamics model. This time-dependency is captured through time-varying model parameters, whose evolution over the MPC prediction horizon is learned using a neural network. Such formulation preserves the computational efficiency and robustness of the base model while equipping it with the capacity to anticipate previously unmodeled phenomena. We evaluated the proposed approach on several challenging systems, including real-world F1TENTH autonomous racing, and demonstrated that it significantly reduces long-horizon prediction errors. Moreover, when integrated within the MPC framework (HyperMPC), our method consistently outperforms existing state-of-the-art techniques.
title: Beyond Constant Parameters: Hyper Prediction Models and HyperMPC

abstract: Training generalist agents is difficult across several axes, requiring us to deal with high-dimensional inputs (space), long horizons (time), and generalization to novel tasks. Recent advances with architectures have allowed for improved scaling along one or two of these axes, but are still computationally prohibitive to use. In this paper, we propose to address all three axes by leveraging \textbf{L}anguage to \textbf{C}ontrol \textbf{D}iffusion models as a hierarchical planner conditioned on language (LCD). We effectively and efficiently scale diffusion models for planning in extended temporal, state, and task dimensions to tackle long horizon control problems conditioned on natural language instructions, as a step towards generalist agents. Comparing LCD with other state-of-the-art models on the CALVIN language robotics benchmark finds that LCD outperforms other SOTA methods in multi-task success rates, whilst improving inference speed over other comparable diffusion models by 3.3x~15x. We show that LCD can successfully leverage the unique strength of diffusion models to produce coherent long range plans while addressing their weakness in generating low-level details and control.
title: Language Control Diffusion: Efficiently Scaling through Space, Time, and Tasks

abstract:
Video prediction models combined with planning algorithms have shown promise
in enabling robots to learn to perform many vision-based tasks through only
self-supervision, reaching novel goals in cluttered scenes with unseen objects.
However, due to the compounding uncertainty in long horizon video prediction
and poor scalability of sampling-based planning optimizers, one significant
limitation of these approaches is the ability to plan over long horizons to
reach distant goals. To that end, we propose a framework for subgoal generation
and planning, hierarchical visual foresight (HVF), which generates subgoal
images conditioned on a goal image, and uses them for planning. The subgoal
images are directly optimized to decompose the task into easy to plan segments,
and as a result, we observe that the method naturally identifies semantically
meaningful states as subgoals. Across three out of four simulated vision-based
manipulation tasks, we find that our method achieves nearly a 200% performance
improvement over planning without subgoals and model-free RL approaches.
Further, our experiments illustrate that our approach extends to real,
cluttered visual scenes. Project page:
this https URL
title: Hierarchical Foresight: Self-Supervised Learning of Long-Horizon Tasks  via Visual Subgoal Generation

abstract:
A robot that learns from demonstrations should not just imitate what it sees
-- it should understand the high-level concepts that are being demonstrated and
generalize them to new tasks. Bilevel planning is a hierarchical model-based
approach where predicates (relational state abstractions) can be leveraged to
achieve compositional generalization. However, previous bilevel planning
approaches depend on predicates that are either hand-engineered or restricted
to very simple forms, limiting their scalability to sophisticated,
high-dimensional state spaces. To address this limitation, we present IVNTR,
the first bilevel planning approach capable of learning neural predicates
directly from demonstrations. Our key innovation is a neuro-symbolic bilevel
learning framework that mirrors the structure of bilevel planning. In IVNTR,
symbolic learning of the predicate "effects" and neural learning of the
predicate "functions" alternate, with each providing guidance for the other. We
evaluate IVNTR in six diverse robot planning domains, demonstrating its
effectiveness in abstracting various continuous and high-dimensional states.
While most existing approaches struggle to generalize (with <35% success rate),
our IVNTR achieves an average of 77% success rate on unseen tasks.
Additionally, we showcase IVNTR on a mobile manipulator, where it learns to
perform real-world mobile manipulation tasks and generalizes to unseen test
scenarios that feature new objects, new states, and longer task horizons. Our
findings underscore the promise of learning and planning with abstractions as a
path towards high-level generalization.

keyInsights: Interleaving symbolic effect vector search with neural network training for predicate classifiers, where the neural loss provides feedback for symbolic decisions, allows for effective invention of expressive and planning-optimized predicates.

The learned neural predicates can sometimes produce more effective planning objectives than even hand-engineered ground-truth effect vectors, highlighting the system's ability to discover non-obvious yet functional abstractions.

Invented neural predicates serve as crucial step-wise success indicators for low-level action samplers, significantly improving the robustness of continuous motion generation and overall system performance.
originalProblem: Traditional imitation learning struggles with generalization to novel, complex tasks requiring compositional reasoning and long action sequences in high-dimensional continuous state spaces.

Bilevel planning, while effective for generalization, is limited by its dependence on either labor-intensive hand-engineered predicates or existing learned predicates that are restrictive or misaligned with planning objectives.

Prior predicate invention methods were constrained to simple, programmatic classifiers, lacking the flexibility and scalability required to abstract sophisticated, high-dimensional continuous states common in robotics.
results: IVNTR achieved an average success rate of 77% on unseen generalization tasks across six diverse simulated robotic domains, outperforming baseline methods that typically scored below 35%.

The framework was successfully deployed on a Boston Dynamics Spot mobile manipulator, demonstrating performance comparable to human-engineered oracle bilevel planners (55-77% success rate vs. 59-83%) on complex real-world mobile manipulation tasks.

Ablation studies confirmed that IVNTR's neural-informed search algorithm was at least 2x more efficient than other search strategies, and using learned predicates for sampler guidance led to performance improvements of up to 98.4%.
solution: IVNTR introduces a "learn-then-select" framework that iteratively interleaves symbolic effect learning with neural classifier training, enabling the discovery of neural predicates directly from demonstrations.

A differentiable loss function is used for neural predicate learning, which is supervised by proposed symbolic effect vectors, allowing the system to abstract continuous and high-dimensional state representations.

An efficient neural-informed search algorithm (a UCT variant) explores the space of effect vectors, guided by the neural network's validation loss, to identify functionally relevant predicates for planning.
paper_summary.summary: IVNTR, a neuro-symbolic bilevel learning framework, automatically invents neural predicates from demonstrations optimized for bilevel planning. This system achieves superior generalization on unseen compositional and sequential tasks across six diverse simulation environments and performs comparably to human-engineered oracle planners on real-world mobile manipulation challenges.

title: Bilevel Learning for Bilevel Planning

abstract: Despite growing interest in active inference for robotic control, its application to complex, long-horizon tasks remains untested. We address this gap by introducing a fully hierarchical active inference architecture for goal-directed behavior in realistic robotic settings. Our model combines a high-level active inference model that selects among discrete skills realized via a whole-body active inference controller. This unified approach enables flexible skill composition, online adaptability, and recovery from task failures without requiring offline training. Evaluated on the Habitat Benchmark for mobile manipulation, our method outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across the three long-horizon tasks, demonstrating for the first time that active inference can scale to the complexity of modern robotics benchmarks.

keyInsights: Active inference, typically applied in simplified settings, can scale to complex, long-horizon mobile manipulation tasks by leveraging a fully hierarchical architecture.
A unified, online inference-driven approach, integrating high-level discrete planning with low-level continuous control, can outperform data-intensive, offline-trained reinforcement learning methods.

The inherent properties of active inference, such as continuous belief updating and explicit retry mechanisms, enable strong generalization and robust online adaptation to task failures without extensive prior training.
originalProblem: Scaling active inference to complex, long-horizon mobile manipulation tasks in realistic environments has been unproven, with prior successes limited to simplified simulations.
Traditional reinforcement learning methods for long-horizon tasks often suffer from high sample complexity, requiring extensive offline training and struggling with generalization and robustness.
Existing mobile manipulation solutions lack inherent mechanisms for online adaptability and recovery from unforeseen circumstances, often relying on pre-trained policies that are brittle to changes.
results: The hierarchical active inference method achieved average completion rates of 72.5% for TidyHouse, 77% for PrepareGroceries, and 50% for SetTable, outperforming the Multi-skill RL Mobile Manipulation baseline.
Across all three Habitat Benchmark tasks, the active inference approach achieved an overall success rate of 66.5%, surpassing the MM baseline's 54.7%.

The proposed method demonstrated strong generalization to unseen layouts and configurations without any offline training, requiring only hand-tuning over a handful of episodes, unlike RL baselines needing millions of steps.
solution: A fully hierarchical active inference architecture is proposed, comprising a high-level POMDP for task orchestration, an intermediate Pick & Place model for robust interaction failures, and a Navigation model for path planning.

Environment understanding is provided by Variational Bayes Gaussian Splatting (VBGS), which constructs a real-time 3D world representation from RGB-D observations for obstacle avoidance and goal representation.

A novel whole-body controller based on Hierarchical Active Inference (HAIF) seamlessly coordinates a mobile base and a 7-DoF arm, integrating arm and base control through shared prediction errors to extend reachability and enable goal-directed behavior with collision avoidance.
paper_summary.summary: This paper from VERSES AI Research Lab presents a fully hierarchical active inference system capable of performing long-horizon mobile manipulation tasks. The system achieves higher success rates on the Habitat Benchmark compared to state-of-the-art reinforcement learning baselines, notably without requiring extensive offline training data.

title: Mobile Manipulation with Active Inference for Long-Horizon Rearrangement Tasks

abstract:
Planning - the ability to analyze the structure of a problem in the large and
decompose it into interrelated subproblems - is a hallmark of human
intelligence. While deep reinforcement learning (RL) has shown great promise
for solving relatively straightforward control tasks, it remains an open
problem how to best incorporate planning into existing deep RL paradigms to
handle increasingly complex environments. One prominent framework, Model-Based
RL, learns a world model and plans using step-by-step virtual rollouts. This
type of world model quickly diverges from reality when the planning horizon
increases, thus struggling at long-horizon planning. How can we learn world
models that endow agents with the ability to do temporally extended reasoning?
In this work, we propose to learn graph-structured world models composed of
sparse, multi-step transitions. We devise a novel algorithm to learn latent
landmarks that are scattered (in terms of reachability) across the goal space
as the nodes on the graph. In this same graph, the edges are the reachability
estimates distilled from Q-functions. On a variety of high-dimensional
continuous control tasks ranging from robotic manipulation to navigation, we
demonstrate that our method, named L3P, significantly outperforms prior work,
and is oftentimes the only method capable of leveraging both the robustness of
model-free RL and generalization of graph-search algorithms. We believe our
work is an important step towards scalable planning in reinforcement learning.
title: World Model as a Graph: Learning Latent Landmarks for Planning

abstract: In robotic domains, learning and planning are complicated by continuous state spaces, continuous action spaces, and long task horizons. In this work, we address these challenges with Neuro-Symbolic Relational Transition Models (NSRTs), a novel class of models that are data-efficient to learn, compatible with powerful robotic planning methods, and generalizable over objects. NSRTs have both symbolic and neural components, enabling a bilevel planning scheme where symbolic AI planning in an outer loop guides continuous planning with neural models in an inner loop. Experiments in four robotic planning domains show that NSRTs can be learned after only tens or hundreds of training episodes, and then used for fast planning in new tasks that require up to 60 actions and involve many more objects than were seen during training. Video: this https URL

organization_info.name: MIT Computer Science and Artificial Intelligence Laboratory

keyInsights: The NSRT framework enables "weak semantics," explicitly acknowledging and handling cases where a symbolic plan may not be continuously executable, which is critical for real-world robotic tasks.
Learning dedicated neural action samplers for each NSRT is crucial for effectively proposing continuous actions that reliably achieve the intended symbolic effects.
Integrating a learned GNN for failure prediction allows the symbolic planner to proactively identify and avoid geometrically impossible or failure-prone abstract paths.
originalProblem: Integrating abstract symbolic planning (effective for long-horizon reasoning) with continuous neural control (necessary for real-world robotic execution) remains a challenge.
Many hierarchical planning systems struggle with "downward refinability," where a symbolically valid plan may not have a physically executable continuous counterpart.
Traditional Task and Motion Planning (TAMP) requires laborious manual specification of symbolic operators, low-level transition models, and action samplers.
results: NSRTs were learned data-efficiently from a few thousand transitions, enabling fast planning for long-horizon tasks (up to 60 actions) within a 3-second timeout.
The approach generalized effectively to new tasks involving significantly more objects than seen during training across PickPlace1D, Kitchen, Blocks, and Painting domains.

Bilevel planning with NSRTs substantially outperformed baselines using only symbolic planning (which failed without downward refinability) or only neural planning (which struggled with long horizons and data efficiency).
solution: Introduces Neuro-Symbolic Relational Transition Models (NSRTs) that combine symbolic components (parameters, preconditions, effects) with neural low-level transition models and action samplers.

Employs a bilevel planning scheme: an outer loop performs A* search for abstract symbolic plans, while an inner loop refines these plans into continuous actions using neural samplers and verifies them via neural transition models.
Learns all NSRT components from transition data, including symbolic operators, neural low-level transition models, neural action samplers, and a graph neural network for failure prediction.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at MIT CSAIL developed a framework featuring Neuro-Symbolic Relational Transition Models (NSRTs) and a bilevel planning strategy, enabling robots to plan for complex, long-horizon tasks in continuous environments. The approach learns all necessary components from limited interaction data, demonstrates robust generalization to novel object configurations, and explicitly addresses the downward refinability problem, outperforming existing baselines across various robotic manipulation domains.

title: Learning Neuro-Symbolic Relational Transition Models for Bilevel Planning

abstract: We present a generative predictive control (GPC) framework that amortizes sampling-based Model Predictive Control (SPC) by bootstrapping it with conditional flow-matching models trained on SPC control sequences collected in simulation. Unlike prior work relying on iterative refinement or gradient-based solvers, we show that meaningful proposal distributions can be learned directly from noisy SPC data, enabling more efficient and informed sampling during online planning. We further demonstrate, for the first time, the application of this approach to real-world contact-rich loco-manipulation with a quadruped robot. Extensive experiments in simulation and on hardware show that our method improves sample efficiency, reduces planning horizon requirements, and generalizes robustly across task variations.

keyInsights: Meaningful generative models for control can be effectively learned directly from noisy control sequences produced by an online SPC algorithm, simplifying the data collection and training pipeline.
Bootstrapping online SPC with offline-learned generative priors combines the efficiency and informed sampling of learned models with the real-time adaptability and robustness of online optimization.

The proposed GPC-CEM strategy, which mixes samples from the learned generative model and the current MPC distribution, enhances search efficiency and exploitation in complex, multimodal action spaces.
originalProblem: Reactive, contact-rich manipulation in high-dimensional state and action spaces poses significant computational challenges for real-time control using methods like Sampling-based Model Predictive Control (SPC).
Traditional SPC methods suffer from high computational costs and sample inefficiency due to the need for numerous forward simulations and extensive exploration of complex search spaces.
Purely offline-trained policies often struggle with out-of-distribution states and lack the real-time adaptability required for dynamic and unstructured environments.
results: GPC-CEM achieved up to 99.8% success rates in simulated contact-rich manipulation tasks (e.g., Push-T), significantly outperforming baselines like standard CEM (85%) and MPPI (62%).

The framework demonstrated strong generalization to unseen task variations (96% success on a Push-K task without retraining) and robustness to reduced planning horizons, maintaining 96% success with a 1-second horizon compared to CEM's 78%.

On a physical Spot quadruped robot, GPC-CEM achieved 60% success in a chair-pushing task, notably outperforming baseline CEM (10% success) by navigating complex obstacles and managing real-world contact dynamics.
solution: Conditional flow-matching models are trained offline to learn high-quality control sequence proposal distributions directly from noisy SPC data generated in simulation.
The learned generative model is integrated into an online Sampling-based MPC (GPC-CEM) to provide informed samples, augmenting the exploration capabilities of the online planner.
A hierarchical control structure is employed for contact-rich loco-manipulation, where GPC plans for high-level torso and arm movements, while a low-level policy handles robot locomotion and balance.
paper_summary.summary: The Generative Predictive Control (GPC) framework enhances Sampling-based Model Predictive Control (SPC) for adaptive contact-rich manipulation by leveraging offline-trained conditional flow-matching models. This approach successfully learns robust control priors directly from noisy simulated SPC data, yielding improved performance and real-world validation on a quadruped robot.

title: Generative Models From and For Sampling-Based MPC: A Bootstrapped Approach For Adaptive Contact-Rich Manipulation

abstract: Long-horizon decision-making with sparse rewards and continuous states and actions remains a fundamental challenge in AI and robotics. Task and motion planning (TAMP) is a model-based framework that addresses this challenge by planning hierarchically with abstract actions (options). These options are manually defined, limiting the agent to behaviors that we as human engineers know how to program (pick, place, move). In this work, we propose Shortcut Learning for Abstract Planning (SLAP), a method that leverages existing TAMP options to automatically discover new ones. Our key idea is to use model-free reinforcement learning (RL) to learn shortcuts in the abstract planning graph induced by the existing options in TAMP. Without any additional assumptions or inputs, shortcut learning leads to shorter solutions than pure planning, and higher task success rates than flat and hierarchical RL. Qualitatively, SLAP discovers dynamic physical improvisations (e.g., slap, wiggle, wipe) that differ significantly from the manually-defined ones. In experiments in four simulated robotic environments, we show that SLAP solves and generalizes to a wide range of tasks, reducing overall plan lengths by over 50% and consistently outperforming planning and RL baselines.
title: SLAP: Shortcut Learning for Abstract Planning

abstract:
Controlling high-dimensional nonlinear systems, such as those found in
biological and robotic applications, is challenging due to large state and
action spaces. While deep reinforcement learning has achieved a number of
successes in these domains, it is computationally intensive and time consuming,
and therefore not suitable for solving large collections of tasks that require
significant manual tuning. In this work, we introduce Model Predictive Control
with Morphology-aware Proportional Control (MPC^2), a hierarchical model-based
learning algorithm for zero-shot and near-real-time control of high-dimensional
complex dynamical systems. MPC^2 uses a sampling-based model predictive
controller for target posture planning, and enables robust control for
high-dimensional tasks by incorporating a morphology-aware proportional
controller for actuator coordination. The algorithm enables motion control of a
high-dimensional human musculoskeletal model in a variety of motion tasks, such
as standing, walking on different terrains, and imitating sports activities.
The reward function of MPC^2 can be tuned via black-box optimization,
drastically reducing the need for human-intensive reward engineering.
title: Motion Control of High-Dimensional Musculoskeletal Systems with  Hierarchical Model-Based Planning

abstract: Robots must understand their environment from raw sensory inputs and reason about the consequences of their actions in it to solve complex tasks. Behavior Cloning (BC) leverages task-specific human demonstrations to learn this knowledge as end-to-end policies. However, these policies are difficult to transfer to new tasks, and generating training data is challenging because it requires careful demonstrations and frequent environment resets. In contrast to such policy-based view, in this paper we take a model-based approach where we collect a few hours of unstructured easy-to-collect play data to learn an action-conditioned visual world model, a diffusion-based action sampler, and optionally a reward model. The world model -- in combination with the action sampler and a reward model -- is then used to optimize long sequences of actions with a Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) planner. The resulting plans are executed on the robot via a zeroth-order Model Predictive Controller (MPC). We show that the action sampler mitigates hallucinations of the world model during planning and validate our approach on 3 real-world robotic tasks with varying levels of planning and modeling complexity. Our experiments support the hypothesis that planning leads to a significant improvement over BC baselines on a standard manipulation test environment.

keyInsights: Diffusion-based visual world models can maintain high fidelity in auto-regressive state predictions for long horizons (over 11 seconds or 64 steps) in complex real-world environments, enabling multi-step planning.
Unstructured, goal-independent 'play data' is sufficient to learn effective world models and action priors, drastically reducing data collection costs compared to explicit task demonstrations.

The combination of MCTS for global reasoning and MPC for robust local control allows robots to synthesize novel behaviors, recover from errors, and achieve high success rates in complex manipulation tasks on physical hardware.
originalProblem: Behavior Cloning (BC) for robotic control requires extensive, high-quality, task-specific demonstrations, struggles to generalize to novel situations, and cannot recover from out-of-distribution states.

Long-horizon planning in real-world robotics using learned visual world models is challenging due to error accumulation in predictions and prohibitive computational requirements for high-fidelity models.
Data collection for robotics is often costly, demanding frequent environment resets and careful curation of goal-directed demonstrations.
results: The diffusion world model consistently predicted future states over 11 seconds (64 steps) with high visual fidelity across rigid, multi-object, and deformable interactions.
WorldPlanner achieved a 97% success rate on the 'push-T' task with learned video rewards, outperforming Diffusion Policy (88%) and ACT (60%) baselines.

The system successfully manipulated deformable objects and showed adaptability to various task objectives (e.g., multi-object interaction) by simply changing the image-conditioned reward function, all trained on a few hours of unstructured data using a single GPU.
solution: A two-stage framework is developed: first, learning a diffusion-based visual world model, a diffusion action generator (action prior), and image-conditioned reward models from unstructured 'play data'.
Global planning is performed using Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS), which leverages the world model for rollouts and the action prior to guide action sampling within the learned dynamics distribution.

A local, zeroth-order Model Predictive Control (MPC) system executes MCTS-generated plans on the real robot in a closed-loop fashion, using the world and reward models for optimization and visual feedback.
paper_summary.summary: WorldPlanner introduces a model-based framework for robotic manipulation that integrates action-conditioned visual world models with Monte Carlo Tree Search and Model Predictive Control. The system demonstrates superior performance over behavior cloning methods on real-world manipulation tasks, learning from only a few hours of unstructured play data.

title: WorldPlanner: Monte Carlo Tree Search and MPC with Action-Conditioned Visual World Models

abstract:
We present Points2Plans, a framework for composable planning with a
relational dynamics model that enables robots to solve long-horizon
manipulation tasks from partial-view point clouds. Given a language instruction
and a point cloud of the scene, our framework initiates a hierarchical planning
procedure, whereby a language model generates a high-level plan and a
sampling-based planner produces constraint-satisfying continuous parameters for
manipulation primitives sequenced according to the high-level plan. Key to our
approach is the use of a relational dynamics model as a unifying interface
between the continuous and symbolic representations of states and actions, thus
facilitating language-driven planning from high-dimensional perceptual input
such as point clouds. Whereas previous relational dynamics models require
training on datasets of multi-step manipulation scenarios that align with the
intended test scenarios, Points2Plans uses only single-step simulated training
data while generalizing zero-shot to a variable number of steps during
real-world evaluations. We evaluate our approach on tasks involving geometric
reasoning, multi-object interactions, and occluded object reasoning in both
simulated and real-world settings. Results demonstrate that Points2Plans offers
strong generalization to unseen long-horizon tasks in the real world, where it
solves over 85% of evaluated tasks while the next best baseline solves only
50%.

keyInsights: Learning delta-dynamics (changes in state) rather than absolute next states significantly improves the robustness and accuracy of long-horizon predictions.
Interleaving latent space dynamics predictions with geometric transformations during rollouts effectively reduces compounding errors, leading to more reliable multi-step planning.

Integrating an LLM for high-level task planning dramatically prunes the search space, enabling planning time to scale linearly with task horizon, and improves logical correctness when informed by feasibility constraints.
originalProblem: Existing Task and Motion Planning (TAMP) systems rely on explicit 3D object models and predefined symbolic operators, limiting application in unstructured environments.
Reinforcement Learning (RL) policy hierarchies struggle with generalization to unseen multi-step tasks and require extensive, task-specific training data.
Prior relational dynamics models require multi-step training data, which increases collection burden, and lack robustness for long-horizon prediction due to compounding errors.
results: Points2Plans achieved over 85% success in zero-shot generalization to unseen 3, 4, and 5-step tasks in simulation and real-world, significantly outperforming baselines (e.g., 50% for next best baseline).
The hybrid rollout strategy and delta-dynamics model demonstrated reduced accumulation of position prediction error compared to purely latent or absolute dynamics models.
An LLM-driven task planner reduced planning time from exponential to linear scaling with task horizon, and its integration with feasibility constraints improved the correctness of generated plans.
solution: A hierarchical planning framework combines a Large Language Model (LLM) for high-level task plan generation with a sampling-based continuous parameter optimizer.
A Composable Relational Dynamics (RD) model, featuring a transformer-based delta-dynamics architecture, predicts changes in object states and relations from single-step data.

A hybrid latent-geometric rollout strategy iteratively predicts latent state changes, applies geometric transformations to point clouds, and re-encodes them, mitigating compounding errors over long horizons.
paper_summary.summary: Points2Plans enables robots to execute complex, long-horizon manipulation tasks from partial-view point clouds and natural language instructions. It achieves over 85% success on unseen multi-step tasks in simulation and real-world scenarios by learning from only single-step demonstrations.

title: Points2Plans: From Point Clouds to Long-Horizon Plans with Composable  Relational Dynamics

abstract: Model Predictive Control (MPC) has established itself as the primary methodology for constrained control, enabling autonomy across diverse applications. While model fidelity is crucial in MPC, solving the corresponding optimization problem in real time remains challenging when combining long horizons with high-fidelity models that capture both short-term dynamics and long-term behavior. Motivated by results on the Exponential Decay of Sensitivities (EDS), which imply that, under certain conditions, the influence of modeling inaccuracies decreases exponentially along the prediction horizon, this paper proposes a multi-timescale MPC scheme for fast-sampled control. Tailored to systems with both fast and slow dynamics, the proposed approach improves computational efficiency by i) switching to a reduced model that captures only the slow, dominant dynamics and ii) exponentially increasing integration step sizes to progressively reduce model detail along the horizon. We evaluate the method on three practically motivated robotic control problems in simulation and observe speed-ups of up to an order of magnitude.
title: Multi-Timescale Model Predictive Control for Slow-Fast Systems

abstract:
An effective approach to solving long-horizon tasks in robotics domains with
continuous state and action spaces is bilevel planning, wherein a high-level
search over an abstraction of an environment is used to guide low-level
decision-making. Recent work has shown how to enable such bilevel planning by
learning abstract models in the form of symbolic operators and neural samplers.
In this work, we show that existing symbolic operator learning approaches fall
short in many robotics domains where a robot's actions tend to cause a large
number of irrelevant changes in the abstract state. This is primarily because
they attempt to learn operators that exactly predict all observed changes in
the abstract state. To overcome this issue, we propose to learn operators that
'choose what to predict' by only modelling changes necessary for abstract
planning to achieve specified goals. Experimentally, we show that our approach
learns operators that lead to efficient planning across 10 different hybrid
robotics domains, including 4 from the challenging BEHAVIOR-100 benchmark,
while generalizing to novel initial states, goals, and objects.

organization_info.name: MIT Computer Science and Artificial Intelligence Laboratory

keyInsights: Optimizing for planning necessity, rather than perfect state prediction, results in significantly simpler, more parsimonious, and generalizable abstract planning models for complex robotics tasks.

The use of quantified delete effects is critical for effectively abstracting away numerous irrelevant state changes in continuous robotic environments, preventing overfitting and improving model robustness.
A bilevel planner's ability to backtrack and evaluate multiple abstract plans (relaxing the downward refinability assumption) is crucial for robust performance in many real-world robotics domains.
originalProblem: Existing operator learning approaches in bilevel planning for robotics optimize for prediction accuracy, often leading to complex, overfit operators that struggle with generalization.
Robot actions in continuous environments frequently cause numerous irrelevant abstract state changes, bloating abstract models and increasing data requirements and planning inefficiency.
Current methods require extensive training data to learn fine-grained models and result in verbose abstract transition models, which slows down high-level planning.
results: Achieved substantially higher success rates on held-out test tasks compared to all baselines across 10 hybrid robotics domains, including complex BEHAVIOR-100 environments, demonstrating superior generalization.
Learned a significantly lower number of operators across various environments, particularly in complex domains, validating the generation of simpler and more parsimonious abstract models.

Demonstrated strong performance with a relatively small number of training demonstrations, indicating high data efficiency, and generally led to comparable or fewer nodes created during high-level planning, improving efficiency.
solution: Develops a hill-climbing search algorithm with a novel objective function that prioritizes learning symbolic operators which model only predicate changes essential for achieving specified goals in abstract planning.

Introduces a new operator semantic where predicted successor states are only required to contain 'necessary atoms,' allowing operators to selectively predict and abstract away incidental state changes.
Incorporates the ability to induce universally quantified single-predicate delete effects, enabling concise representation of broad state changes without explicitly enumerating every affected object.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from MIT CSAIL and Meta AI introduce a method for learning abstract planning models that selectively predict only necessary state changes for efficient bilevel planning in robotics. This approach yields substantially higher success rates on held-out test tasks across 10 hybrid robotics domains, including BEHAVIOR-100 environments, by generating simpler and more generalizable symbolic operators.

title: Learning Efficient Abstract Planning Models that Choose What to Predict

abstract: World models for environments with many objects face a combinatorial explosion of states: as the number of objects increases, the number of possible arrangements grows exponentially. In this paper, we learn to generalize over robotic pick-and-place tasks using object-factored world models, which combat the combinatorial explosion by ensuring that predictions are equivariant to permutations of objects. Previous object-factored models were limited either by their inability to model actions, or by their inability to plan for complex manipulation tasks. We build on recent contrastive methods for training object-factored world models, which we extend to model continuous robot actions and to accurately predict the physics of robotic pick-and-place. To do so, we use a residual stack of graph neural networks that receive action information at multiple levels in both their node and edge neural networks. Crucially, our learned model can make predictions about tasks not represented in the training data. That is, we demonstrate successful zero-shot generalization to novel tasks, with only a minor decrease in model performance. Moreover, we show that an ensemble of our models can be used to plan for tasks involving up to 12 pick and place actions using heuristic search. We also demonstrate transfer to a physical robot.
title: Factored World Models for Zero-Shot Generalization in Robotic Manipulation

abstract:
Nonprehensile manipulation, such as pushing objects across cluttered environments, presents a challenging control problem due to complex contact dynamics and long-horizon planning requirements. In this work, we propose HeRD, a hierarchical reinforcement learning-diffusion policy that decomposes pushing tasks into two levels: high-level goal selection and low-level trajectory generation. We employ a high-level reinforcement learning (RL) agent to select intermediate spatial goals, and a low-level goal-conditioned diffusion model to generate feasible, efficient trajectories to reach them.
This architecture combines the long-term reward maximizing behaviour of RL with the generative capabilities of diffusion models. We evaluate our method in a 2D simulation environment and show that it outperforms the state-of-the-art baseline in success rate, path efficiency, and generalization across multiple environment configurations. Our results suggest that hierarchical control with generative low-level planning is a promising direction for scalable, goal-directed nonprehensile manipulation. Code, documentation, and trained models are available: this https URL.
title: Push Smarter, Not Harder: Hierarchical RL-Diffusion Policy for Efficient Nonprehensile Manipulation

abstract:
The simulation-to-real gap problem and the high computational burden of
whole-body Model Predictive Control (whole-body MPC) continue to present
challenges in generating a wide variety of movements using whole-body MPC for
real humanoid robots. This paper presents a biologically-inspired hierarchical
learning framework as a potential solution to the aforementioned problems. The
proposed three-layer hierarchical framework enables the generation of
multi-contact, dynamic behaviours even with low-frequency policy updates of
whole-body MPC. The upper layer is responsible for learning an accurate
dynamics model with the objective of reducing the discrepancy between the
analytical model and the real system. This enables the computation of effective
control policies using whole-body MPC. Subsequently, the middle and lower
layers are tasked with learning additional policies to generate high-frequency
control inputs. In order to learn an accurate dynamics model in the upper
layer, an augmented model using a deep residual network is trained by
model-based reinforcement learning with stochastic whole-body MPC. The proposed
framework was evaluated in 10 distinct motion learning scenarios, including
jogging on a flat surface and skating on curved surfaces. The results
demonstrate that a wide variety of motions can be successfully generated on a
real humanoid robot using whole-body MPC through learning with the proposed
framework.
title: Hierarchical Learning Framework for Whole-Body Model Predictive Control  of a Real Humanoid Robot

abstract: Solving robotic navigation tasks via reinforcement learning (RL) is challenging due to their sparse reward and long decision horizon nature. However, in many navigation tasks, high-level (HL) task representations, like a rough floor plan, are available. Previous work has demonstrated efficient learning by hierarchal approaches consisting of path planning in the HL representation and using sub-goals derived from the plan to guide the RL policy in the source task. However, these approaches usually neglect the complex dynamics and sub-optimal sub-goal-reaching capabilities of the robot during planning. This work overcomes these limitations by proposing a novel hierarchical framework that utilizes a trainable planning policy for the HL representation. Thereby robot capabilities and environment conditions can be learned utilizing collected rollout data. We specifically introduce a planning policy based on value iteration with a learned transition model (VI-RL). In simulated robotic navigation tasks, VI-RL results in consistent strong improvement over vanilla RL, is on par with vanilla hierarchal RL on single layouts but more broadly applicable to multiple layouts, and is on par with trainable HL path planning baselines except for a parking task with difficult non-holonomic dynamics where it shows marked improvements.

keyInsights: Hierarchically combining planning in an abstract representation with goal-conditioned reinforcement learning significantly increases data-efficiency for long-horizon, sparse-reward tasks.
Learning a high-level transition model that reflects the robot's capabilities is essential for effective planning when environmental factors (like terrain) or complex dynamics are present.

Transition models must consider both the high-level state and the chosen high-level action (sub-goal) to effectively model non-holonomic constraints, as purely state-dependent models are insufficient.
originalProblem: Complex robotic navigation tasks are challenging for traditional Reinforcement Learning due to sparse rewards and long decision horizons.
Existing Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning (HRL) methods often struggle with generalizing effectively across multiple task instances or environment layouts.

Prior hierarchical and planning approaches frequently neglect the actual, often sub-optimal, execution capabilities of the low-level policy and the robot's complex dynamics during high-level planning.
results: Hierarchical methods (VI-RL, MVPROP-RL) achieved 100% goal-reaching in the Four Rooms environment, while flat vanilla RL consistently failed, demonstrating superior data-efficiency.
In environments with challenging terrain, VI-RL with a learned transition model maintained 100% goal-reaching, whereas optimistic shortest-path planning (VI-RL OM) dropped below 20% success.
VI-RL achieved over 80% goal-reaching probability in the non-holonomic Vehicle Parking task, significantly outperforming all baselines by effectively modeling action-dependent dynamics.
solution: A hierarchical framework is proposed that combines abstract high-level planning (e.g., using floor plans) with sub-goal-guided reinforcement learning in continuous low-level state spaces.
The framework introduces a trainable high-level planning policy that adapts to the low-level policy's performance and environmental conditions by learning from collected rollout data.
A specific instance, VI-RL, employs Value Iteration in the high-level state space using a learned transition model that explicitly considers the dynamics induced by the low-level policy.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Bosch Center for AI and UvA-Bosch Delta Lab developed a hierarchical robot navigation framework integrating high-level planning with low-level reinforcement learning, featuring a trainable high-level policy. This framework, exemplified by VI-RL, robustly navigates complex, sparse-reward environments and effectively learns to adapt to challenging robot dynamics and environmental conditions, outperforming non-hierarchical methods and existing HRL baselines.

title: Hierarchies of Planning and Reinforcement Learning for Robot Navigation

abstract:
We present a hierarchical planning and control framework that enables an
agent to perform various tasks and adapt to a new task flexibly. Rather than
learning an individual policy for each particular task, the proposed framework,
DISH, distills a hierarchical policy from a set of tasks by representation and
reinforcement learning. The framework is based on the idea of latent variable
models that represent high-dimensional observations using low-dimensional
latent variables. The resulting policy consists of two levels of hierarchy: (i)
a planning module that reasons a sequence of latent intentions that would lead
to an optimistic future and (ii) a feedback control policy, shared across the
tasks, that executes the inferred intention. Because the planning is performed
in low-dimensional latent space, the learned policy can immediately be used to
solve or adapt to new tasks without additional training. We demonstrate the
proposed framework can learn compact representations (3- and 1-dimensional
latent states and commands for a humanoid with 197- and 36-dimensional state
features and actions) while solving a small number of imitation tasks, and the
resulting policy is directly applicable to other types of tasks, i.e.,
navigation in cluttered environments. Video: this https URL
title: Distilling a Hierarchical Policy for Planning and Control via  Representation and Reinforcement Learning

abstract: Learning robust robotic control policies remains a major challenge due to the high cost of collecting labeled data, limited generalization to unseen environments, and difficulties in planning over long horizons. While Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models offer a promising solution by grounding natural language instructions into single-step control commands, they often lack mechanisms for lookahead and struggle with compounding errors in dynamic tasks. In this project, we introduce Scaling Inference-Time COMpute for VLAs (SITCOM), a framework that augments any pretrained VLA with model-based rollouts and reward-based trajectory selection, inspired by Model Predictive Control algorithm. SITCOM leverages a learned dynamics model to simulate multi-step action rollouts to select the best candidate plan for real-world execution, transforming one-shot VLAs into robust long-horizon planners. We develop an efficient transformer-based dynamics model trained on large-scale BridgeV2 data and fine-tuned on SIMPLER environments to bridge the Real2Sim gap, and score candidate rollouts using rewards from simulator. Through comprehensive evaluation across multiple tasks and settings in the SIMPLER environment, we demonstrate that SITCOM when combined with a good reward function can significantly improve task completion rate from 48% to 72% using trained dynamics model.
title: SITCOM: Scaling Inference-Time COMpute for VLAs

abstract:
We present HiDe, a novel hierarchical reinforcement learning architecture
that successfully solves long horizon control tasks and generalizes to unseen
test scenarios. Functional decomposition between planning and low-level control
is achieved by explicitly separating the state-action spaces across the
hierarchy, which allows the integration of task-relevant knowledge per layer.
We propose an RL-based planner to efficiently leverage the information in the
planning layer of the hierarchy, while the control layer learns a
goal-conditioned control policy. The hierarchy is trained jointly but allows
for the modular transfer of policy layers across hierarchies of different
agents. We experimentally show that our method generalizes across unseen test
environments and can scale to 3x horizon length compared to both learning and
non-learning based methods. We evaluate on complex continuous control tasks
with sparse rewards, including navigation and robot manipulation.
title: Learning Functionally Decomposed Hierarchies for Continuous Control  Tasks with Path Planning

abstract: The fundamental challenge of planning for multi-step manipulation is to find effective and plausible action sequences that lead to the task goal. We present Cascaded Variational Inference (CAVIN) Planner, a model-based method that hierarchically generates plans by sampling from latent spaces. To facilitate planning over long time horizons, our method learns latent representations that decouple the prediction of high-level effects from the generation of low-level motions through cascaded variational inference. This enables us to model dynamics at two different levels of temporal resolutions for hierarchical planning. We evaluate our approach in three multi-step robotic manipulation tasks in cluttered tabletop environments given high-dimensional observations. Empirical results demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms state-of-the-art model-based methods by strategically interacting with multiple objects.

keyInsights: Multi-step manipulation tasks exhibit an inherent hierarchical structure that can be exploited by decoupling high-level desired effects (subgoals) from low-level motion generation.

Learning and planning within distinct latent spaces for effects and motions allows for efficient pruning of unfavorable high-level trajectories, thereby reducing the search space for long-horizon problems.
An end-to-end cascaded variational inference framework enables joint learning of these hierarchical latent dynamics solely from task-agnostic, self-supervised data.
originalProblem: Robotic manipulation tasks involving multiple steps often require long-horizon planning in high-dimensional visual environments.
Existing "flat" planning approaches struggle with the combinatorial explosion of the action space and the curse of dimensionality over long time horizons.
Current methods are limited by uncertainties in visual perception and the inability to effectively bridge high-level task reasoning with low-level motion execution in complex settings.
results: The CAVIN Planner achieved consistently superior performance over model-based baselines across three multi-step tasks, with success rates improving by over 10% in dense reward settings and more significantly (up to 20.1%) with sparse rewards.

The method demonstrated enhanced robustness to increasing scene complexity, experiencing a performance drop of only 9.4% in the Clearing task when objects increased from 1 to 5, compared to 51.1-70.2% for baselines.

Models trained purely in simulation successfully transferred to real-world robotic tasks without adaptation, achieving high success rates such as 93.3% in Clearing, 73.3% in Insertion, and 80.0% in Crossing.
solution: A model-based hierarchical planning algorithm, the CAVIN Planner, is introduced, operating with two levels of abstraction in learned latent spaces.
High-level planning defines subgoals (effects) using a Latent Effect Space, while low-level planning generates fine-grained motion sequences using a Latent Motion Space.
The system learns its dynamics and latent representations end-to-end from task-agnostic self-supervised data through a cascaded variational inference framework.
paper_summary.summary: The CAVIN Planner introduces a model-based hierarchical planning algorithm for multi-step robotic manipulation, employing cascaded variational inference to learn latent representations of high-level effects and low-level motions. It demonstrates superior performance and robustness in complex, long-horizon tasks across varying scene complexities and reward structures, with successful zero-shot transfer from simulation to real-world environments.

title: Dynamics Learning with Cascaded Variational Inference for Multi-Step Manipulation

abstract: Long-horizon embodied planning is challenging because the world does not only change through an agent's actions: exogenous processes (e.g., water heating, dominoes cascading) unfold concurrently with the agent's actions.
